### 3.2 Derivation: Best-Fit Circle from Three Points (Diameter Formula)

**Problem Statement:** A CMM probes three points on a circular feature: $P_1, P_2, P_3$. Derive the diameter formula $D = \dfrac{abc}{4A}$ where $a, b, c$ are the chord lengths and $A$ is the area of the triangle $P_1P_2P_3$.

**Step 1 — Define Side Lengths**

By the Euclidean distance formula derived above:

$$a = \overline{P_2P_3}, \quad b = \overline{P_1P_3}, \quad c = \overline{P_1P_2}$$

**Step 2 — Area of the Triangle (Heron's Formula)**

$$A = \sqrt{ s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c) } \quad \text{where} \quad s = \dfrac{a+b+c}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Inscribed Circle Theorem**

The circle passing through three non-collinear points has a circumradius $R$:

$$R = \dfrac{abc}{4A}$$

**Step 4 — Diameter is Twice the Radius**

$$D = 2R = \dfrac{abc}{2A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on KTU Convention:** Some textbooks cite the formula as $D = \dfrac{abc}{4A}$ for the *radius* (not diameter). Always check whether the textbook defines $D$ as diameter or radius. In PEMRT633 references, $D$ denotes **diameter**, so the correct expression is $D = \dfrac{abc}{2A}$.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: CMM Accuracy Verification

**Problem:** A CMM has the manufacturer-specified accuracy equation $E = E_0 + E_1 \cdot L$ with $E_0 = 1.9\,\mu$m and $E_1 = 2.5\,\mu$m/m. Find the maximum permissible error when measuring a length of 400 mm.

**Step 1 — Convert Units Consistently**

$$E_1 = 2.5 \, \mu\text{m/m} = 0.0025 \, \mu\text{m/mm}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the ISO 10360 Equation**

$$E = 1.9 + (0.0025)(400) = 1.9 + 1.0 = 2.9 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

The CMM is guaranteed to measure a 400 mm length within $\pm 2.9\,\mu$m of the true value — making it suitable for high-precision industries such as aerospace and medical implants.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CMM vs Other Metrology Methods

| Parameter | **CMM** | **Caliper / Micrometer** | **Optical Comparator** |
|-----------|---------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| **Dimensional Range** | Up to several meters | < 300 mm typical | < 200 mm |
| **Accuracy** | 1–5 µm | 1–10 µm | 5–20 µm |
| **3D Capability** | Yes (full 3D) | No (1D/2D) | Limited (2D profile) |
| **Automation** | High (CNC) | Manual | Semi |
| **Data Output** | Digital CAD-compatible | Manual | Image / manual |
| **Cost** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Best Use Case** | Complex 3D parts | Simple lengths | 2D outline profiles |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CMM System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ctrlSys[CONTROL SUBSYSTEM]
        cmmA[Machine Controller CPU]
        cmmB[Motion Drive Card]
        cmmC[Encoder Interface Module]
    end

    subgraph mechaSys[MECHANICAL SUBSYSTEM]
        cmmD[X Axis Drive and Scale]
        cmmE[Y Axis Drive and Scale]
        cmmF[Z Axis Drive and Scale]
        cmmG[Bridge or Column Structure]
    end

    subgraph probeSys[PROBE SUBSYSTEM]
        cmmH[Probe Head and Stylus]
        cmmI[Probe Signal Conditioner]
        cmmJ[Sensor Type Module]
    end

    subgraph softSys[SOFTWARE SUBSYSTEM]
        cmmK[Measurement Routine Engine]
        cmmL[GD&T Tolerance Evaluator]
        cmmM[Report and CAD Output Generator]
    end

    subgraph workSys[WORKPIECE ZONE]
        cmmN[Fixture and Clamping Table]
        cmmO[Part Under Inspection]
    end

    cmmA --> cmmB
    cmmA --> cmmC
    cmmB --> cmmD
    cmmB --> cmmE
    cmmB --> cmmF
    cmmD --> cmmG
    cmmE --> cmmG
    cmmF --> cmmG
    cmmC --> cmmI
    cmmI --> cmmH
    cmmH --> cmmO
    cmmN --> cmmO
    cmmA --> cmmK
    cmmH --> cmmI
    cmmI --> cmmK
    cmmK --> cmmL
    cmmL --> cmmM
```

### 4.2 CMM Operational Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode([Start Inspection]) --> loadPart[Load Part on Fixture]
    loadPart --> calProbe[Calibrate Probe with Datum Sphere]
    calProbe --> defineAlign[Define Coordinate System and Datum Alignment]
    defineAlign --> runRoutine[Execute Measurement Routine]
    runRoutine --> probePoint[Probe Contacts Surface and Captures X Y Z]
    probePoint --> storePoint[Store Point in Memory Buffer]
    storePoint --> checkMore{More Points to Probe?}
    checkMore -- Yes --> runRoutine
    checkMore -- No --> computeGeo[Compute Geometric Features and Tolerances]
    computeGeo --> compareGDNT[Compare Against GD&T Specifications]
    compareGDNT --> passFail{Part Pass or Fail?}
    passFail -- Pass --> reportOK[Generate Quality Pass Report]
    passFail -- Fail --> reportNG[Generate Quality Reject Report with Defect Map]
    reportOK --> endNode([End])
    reportNG --> endNode
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — CMM Applications Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appRoot[CMM Application Domains]

    appRoot --> aeroNode[Aerospace Engine Blade Inspection]
    appRoot --> autoNode[Automotive Engine Block Verification]
    appRoot --> medNode[Medical Implant Quality Control]
    appRoot --> moldNode[Tool and Die Verification]
    appRoot --> revNode[Reverse Engineering and Legacy Part Digitization]
    appRoot --> elecNode[Electronics Enclosure Form Check]

    aeroNode --> out1[Sub 100 Micrometer Tolerance]
    autoNode --> out2[Inline 100 Percent Inspection]
    medNode --> out3[ISO 13485 Compliance Output]
    moldNode --> out4[First Article Inspection Report]
    revNode --> out5[Point Cloud to CAD Conversion]
    elecNode --> out6[Statistical Process Control Feed]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define a Coordinate Measuring Machine. Mention any two types of CMMs based on their structure.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM) is a precision metrology device that measures the geometry of a physical object by determining the spatial coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ of points on its surface using a probe, and computes dimensional and form characteristics through software.

**Two types based on structure:**
1. **Bridge-type CMM** — Most common; uses a moving bridge along the X-axis carrying the Y and Z axes. Suitable for medium-sized parts.
2. **Cantilever-type CMM** — Has a single supporting column, offering better access to the workpiece from three sides. Ideal for large, sheet-metal-like parts.

*(Other acceptable types: Gantry-type, Horizontal-arm, Column-type.)* **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2. List any four advantages of using a CMM over conventional hand-measuring tools.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Higher Accuracy** — Sub-micrometer precision, far superior to calipers or micrometers.
2. **Automation Compatibility** — Can be integrated into CNC production lines for unmanned inspection.
3. **Versatility** — Measures 3D complex geometries (holes, slots, curved surfaces) that hand tools cannot.
4. **Digital Data Output** — Generates electronic inspection reports compatible with CAD/CAM systems, enabling traceability and SPC (Statistical Process Control). **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a Coordinate Measuring Machine with a neat functional block diagram. Discuss the role of the probe, scales, and software in measurement. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A CMM works on the principle of **Cartesian coordinate measurement**:
1. The workpiece is placed on a granite or steel table (which serves as a stable reference plane).
2. A **probe** (touch-trigger, scanning, or non-contact) is mounted on the Z-axis of the machine.
3. The probe is moved to contact the workpiece surface; at the instant of contact, the **linear encoders (scales)** on the X, Y, Z axes record the exact position.
4. This generates a set of discrete 3D points $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$.
5. The **software module** processes these points to compute distances, diameters, angles, GD&T parameters, and compare them with tolerances.

**Role of Key Subsystems:**
- **Probe:** Acts as the "sensor" — converts physical contact/non-contact into an electrical trigger.
- **Scales (Encoders):** Provide the position feedback with resolution up to **0.1 µm** for X, Y, Z axes.
- **Software:** Performs geometric fitting (least-squares), computes GD&T, and generates inspection reports.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for functional architecture.)* **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b)** Discuss the major applications of CMMs in aerospace, automotive, and medical-device industries. Explain how CMMs contribute to Industry 4.0 and smart manufacturing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Applications:**
- **Aerospace:** Inspection of turbine blades, wing components, and structural assemblies where tolerances are sub-100 µm; first-article inspection (FAI) of engine casings.
- **Automotive:** Inline inspection of engine blocks, transmission housings, and chassis components in high-volume production lines; SPC data feeds back to machining centres for closed-loop quality control.
- **Medical Devices:** Verification of orthopedic implants (hip stems, knee joints), surgical instruments, and dental prosthetics as per **ISO 13485** quality standards.

**Contribution to Industry 4.0:**
- **IoT Integration:** CMMs share real-time inspection data with MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems).
- **Closed-Loop Automation:** Defect data triggers automatic rework or machine parameter correction.
- **Digital Twin Validation:** CMM data validates 3D digital twins of physical parts.
- **AI-Based Defect Prediction:** Historical CMM data trains machine learning models for predictive quality. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the different types of CMMs based on their construction. Compare the bridge-type and cantilever-type CMMs. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of CMMs based on construction:**

1. **Bridge-type CMM:** Has a movable bridge moving along the X-axis, with Y-axis mounted on the bridge and Z-axis carrying the probe. Provides high rigidity and accuracy; widely used in production environments.

2. **Cantilever-type CMM:** Has a single vertical column on one side; the Y-arm extends horizontally. Allows easy loading/unloading of large parts from three sides but has lower rigidity.

3. **Gantry-type CMM:** Bridge spans across the table supported at both ends; ideal for very large parts (e.g., aircraft wings, ship propellers).

4. **Horizontal-arm CMM:** Probe moves on a horizontal arm; used for thin parts like sheet metal.

5. **Column-type CMM:** Vertical column-mounted arm moves in Y-axis; simple design for small parts.

**Comparison: Bridge vs Cantilever**

| Parameter | Bridge-Type | Cantilever-Type |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| Rigidity | High | Lower |
| Loading Access | Restricted to 2 sides | 3 sides open |
| Accuracy | High (1.5–3 µm) | Moderate (3–5 µm) |
| Best For | Medium parts | Large thin parts |
| Cost | Higher | Moderate |

**[7 Marks]**

---

**(b)** A CMM is rated with an accuracy specification $E = E_0 + E_1 \cdot L$ where $E_0 = 2.0\,\mu$m and $E_1 = 3.0\,\mu$m/m. Calculate the maximum permissible error for measuring: (i) a length of 100 mm, and (ii) a length of 500 mm. Comment on how temperature affects CMM accuracy. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $E_0 = 2.0\,\mu$m, $E_1 = 3.0\,\mu$m/m $= 0.003\,\mu$m/mm.

**(i) For L = 100 mm:**

$$E_1 \cdot L = 0.003 \times 100 = 0.3 \, \mu\text{m}$$

$$E = 2.0 + 0.3 = 2.3 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**(ii) For L = 500 mm:**

$$E_1 \cdot L = 0.003 \times 500 = 1.5 \, \mu\text{m}$$

$$E = 2.0 + 1.5 = 3.5 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Comment on Temperature Effects:**

Temperature significantly affects CMM accuracy because of **thermal expansion** of the machine structure (typically steel: $\alpha \approx 11.5 \times 10^{-6}\,/^{\circ}\text{C}$) and the workpiece.

The corrected length is given by:

$$L_{corrected} = L \cdot \left[ 1 + \alpha \cdot (\Delta T) \right]$$

For a 500 mm steel part with a $1^{\circ}\text{C}$ error:

$$\Delta L = 500 \times 11.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 = 5.75 \, \mu\text{m}$$

This is **larger than the CMM's own permissible error**, hence:
- CMMs are housed in **temperature-controlled rooms** at **$20 \pm 0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$**.
- Workpieces are **acclimatized** before measurement.
- High-end CMMs use **ceramic or Invar scales** (low $\alpha$) to minimize thermal drift. **[7 Marks]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in CMM Questions:**
> 1. **Formula Confusion:** Students often write $D = \dfrac{abc}{4A}$ (which is the *radius* $R$ in circumcircle geometry) when the question asks for **diameter** — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert $E_1$ from µm/m to µm/mm before substituting into the equation — leading to a numerical answer off by a factor of 1000.
> 3. **Skipping Datum Definition:** In GD&T-based questions, students compute features but forget to define the **datum reference frame** (DRF) — losing 2–3 marks in (a) parts.
> 4. **Confusing Probe Types:** Mixing up "touch-trigger probe" (discrete points) with "scanning probe" (continuous points) — examiners deduct for mislabelled diagrams.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A CMM is a 3D Cartesian metrology device that captures $(X, Y, Z)$ coordinates using a probe and computes geometry via software (per **ISO 10360-1**).
- **Core Subsystems:** Mechanical structure (axes), Probe, Scales/Encoders, Controller, Software.
- **Key Formula:** ISO 10360-2 accuracy: $E = E_0 + E_1 \cdot L$.
- **Probe Types:** Touch-trigger, Scanning (analog), Laser, Vision — each suited to different applications.
- **Structural Types:** Bridge, Cantilever, Gantry, Horizontal-arm, Column.
- **Distance Formula:** $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}$ — essential for KTU derivations.
- **Circle Diameter (3-point):** $D = \dfrac{abc}{2A}$ (verify with the textbook convention used in your module).
- **Thermal Compensation:** $L_{corrected} = L \cdot [1 + \alpha \cdot \Delta T]$; standard reference temperature is **$20^{\circ}\text{C}$**.
- **Industry 4.0 Link:** CMMs are integral to **closed-loop quality control**, **digital twins**, and **predictive analytics** in smart factories.
- **Standards to Remember:** **ISO 10360** (CMM testing), **ASME Y14.5 / ISO 1101** (GD&T), **ISO 13485** (medical device QA).
- **High-Yield Applications:** Aerospace (turbine blades), Automotive (engine blocks), Medical (implants), Tool & Die (moulds), Reverse Engineering (legacy digitization).
- **Common Mistake in Exam:** Forgetting to mention the **software role** in converting raw coordinates into GD&T-compliant reports — this omission typically costs 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Vision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT633 - ADVANCED AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machine Vision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Vision (MV)** is an engineering discipline that integrates optical sensing, digital image processing, embedded computing, and artificial intelligence to automatically inspect, identify, measure, guide, and control industrial processes by interpreting visual information extracted from the physical world.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Advanced Automation (PEMRT633)**, Machine Vision is formally defined as a *non-contact, optical-based sensory subsystem* of a flexible automation cell that converts illumination patterns reflected from a part surface into structured numerical data, executes multi-stage digital transformations, and outputs actuator-level control signals or decisions conforming to a programmed inspection criterion.

**Key Constituent Standards / Metrics:**
- **Resolution:** measured in **pixels (px)** or **megapixels (MP)**
- **Field of View (FOV):** in **millimetres (mm)** or **metres (m)**
- **Pixel Resolution:** $R_{px} = \dfrac{FOV}{N_{sensor}}$ in **mm/pixel**
- **Frame Rate:** in **frames per second (fps)**
- **Working Distance (WD):** in **mm**
- **Depth of Field (DOF):** in **mm**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Superhuman Quality Inspector"**
>
> Imagine a human inspector on a production line who checks 2,000 biscuits per hour for broken edges, wrong colour, and wrong shape. The human uses *eyes* (optics), *brain* (processor), and *memory* (database). After a few hours the human becomes tired, inconsistent, and makes mistakes.
>
> A **Machine Vision System** is a tireless digital version of this inspector:
> - The **camera** is the eye.
> - The **lighting** is the controllable daylight (machines can see better with optimal lighting).
> - The **processor** is the brain that runs algorithms.
> - The **decision logic** is the rulebook the inspector follows.
> - The **output relay** is the hand that sorts "OK" vs "Reject".
>
> It inspects every product with the *same accuracy*, at *production-line speed*, *24/7*, without fatigue.

---

## 1.3 Why Machine Vision is Critical in Advanced Automation

In a **Cyber-Physical Manufacturing System (CPMS)**, Machine Vision acts as the *closed-loop perceptual bridge* between the digital twin and the physical assembly line. The KTU 2024 syllabus positions it as the enabling technology behind:

- **Closed-loop visual servoing** for robotic manipulators
- **100% inline inspection** (Zero-Defect Manufacturing – ZDM)
- **Bin-picking** using 3D point-cloud interpretation
- **AGV/AMR navigation** via SLAM with vision sensors
- **Additive manufacturing in-situ monitoring**

---

## 1.4 Hierarchical Classification of Machine Vision Tasks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU classifies MV operations into a **6-tier hierarchy** (from low-level data to high-level cognition):

| Tier | Task | Example |
|:----:|:-----|:--------|
| 1 | Image Acquisition | Capture frame from CMOS sensor |
| 2 | Pre-processing | Noise filtering, illumination correction |
| 3 | Segmentation | Isolate the blob of interest |
| 4 | Feature Extraction | Edges, corners, blobs, area |
| 5 | Pattern Recognition / Classification | Match blob to template |
| 6 | Decision & Actuation | Reject / Accept / Pick |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Machine Vision System

A Machine Vision system is not a single device — it is a **pipeline of cooperating sub-systems**. Understanding each block is essential for the 14-mark KTU questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5-Stage MV Pipeline (Mandatory KTU Architecture)**

**Stage 1 — Illumination Subsystem**
- Provides controlled, repeatable, and uniform lighting on the part.
- Common types:
    - **Backlighting** → silhouette / dimensional gauging
    - **Diffuse dome lighting** → curved / specular surfaces
    - **Ring lighting** → general coaxial illumination
    - **Structured light** → 3D profiling
    - **Strobe / pulsed LED** → freezing fast motion
- The lighting must dominate ambient light; otherwise vision becomes non-deterministic.

**Stage 2 — Optics Subsystem (Lens)**
- The lens focuses reflected light onto the image sensor.
- Key parameter is the **focal length (f)** in mm, related by the *thin-lens equation*:

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{s_o} + \frac{1}{s_i}$$

where $s_o$ = object distance, $s_i$ = image distance.

- **Depth of Field (DOF)** for a vision system:

$$DOF = 2 \cdot N \cdot c \cdot \left(1 + \frac{M}{1+M}\right)^2$$

where $N$ = f-number, $c$ = circle of confusion, $M$ = magnification.

**Stage 3 — Image Sensor Subsystem**
- Converts incident photons into a 2D array of digital pixel values.
- Two main technologies:
    - **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device)** — high uniformity, low noise
    - **CMOS (Active Pixel Sensor)** — high speed, low power, on-chip processing
- Output is a *grayscale intensity map* $I(x, y) \in [0, 255]$ or a colour image as 3 channels $I_R, I_G, I_B$.

**Stage 4 — Image Processing & Analysis Subsystem**
- This is the *software brain* of the system.
- Implements pre-processing → segmentation → feature extraction → classification.
- Runs on:
    - **Smart cameras** (embedded ARM/DSP)
    - **PC-based frame grabbers** + GPU/CPU
    - **Industrial PCs** with real-time OS
- Algorithms include filtering, thresholding, morphology, edge detection, Hough transform, template matching, and ML-based CNN classifiers.

**Stage 5 — Decision / Communication Subsystem**
- Outputs control signals over industrial fieldbuses:
    - **Digital I/O** (24 V PLC signals) — discrete pass/fail
    - **EtherNet/IP, PROFINET, EtherCAT** — networked data
    - **TCP/IP, MQTT, OPC-UA** — Industry 4.0 / IIoT protocols
- Communicates decisions to:
    - **PLC** (Programmable Logic Controller)
    - **Robot controller**
    - **SCADA / MES** (Manufacturing Execution System)

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | Why this stage exists | Engineering justification |
|:------|:---------------------|:-------------------------|
| Lighting | Images of the same part can vary 100× under different illumination | Standardises input → repeatable processing |
| Optics | Maps scene to sensor with correct size and focus | Controls magnification, perspective, DOF |
| Sensor | Photons must be digitised | Bridges analogue world to digital processor |
| Processing | Raw pixels carry noise, redundancy, irrelevant info | Distils pixels into measurable features |
| Decision | Manufacturing needs an actionable Yes/No signal | Closes the automation feedback loop |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorise equation bank** for Machine Vision. Every KTU Part-B (14 mark) question expects at least 2 of these in your answer script.

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:-------------------|:------|
| 1 | Thin-lens equation | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{s_o} + \dfrac{1}{s_i}$ | $f$=focal length, $s_o$=object dist., $s_i$=image dist. | mm |
| 2 | Magnification | $M = \dfrac{h_i}{h_o} = \dfrac{s_i}{s_o}$ | $h_i$=image height, $h_o$=object height | dimensionless |
| 3 | Sensor pixel size | $p = \dfrac{W_{sensor}}{N_{pixels}}$ | $W_{sensor}$=sensor width, $N_{pixels}$=pixel count | $\mu m$ |
| 4 | Pixel resolution (object space) | $R_{px} = \dfrac{FOV}{N_{pixels}}$ | FOV=field of view, $N_{pixels}$=pixels along axis | mm/px |
| 5 | Measurement accuracy | $\delta = \pm R_{px} \cdot k$ | $k$=sub-pixel factor (typically 1/4 to 1/10) | mm |
| 6 | Shutter / motion blur | $v_{blur} = \dfrac{v_{part}}{f_{frame} \cdot R_{px}}$ | $v_{part}$=part velocity | mm |
| 7 | 2D convolution | $g(x,y) = \sum_{u=-a}^{a}\sum_{v=-b}^{b} f(x-u, y-v) \cdot h(u,v)$ | $f$=input image, $h$=kernel, $g$=output | intensity |
| 8 | Image gradient (Sobel) | $\nabla I = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ | $G_x$, $G_y$ = directional gradients | intensity/px |
| 9 | Otsu's intra-class variance | $\sigma_w^2(t) = w_0 \sigma_0^2 + w_1 \sigma_1^2$ | $w_i$=class weights, $\sigma_i^2$=variances | intensity$^2$ |
| 10 | Hough line param. | $\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$ | $\rho$=radial distance, $\theta$=angle | px, rad |
| 11 | Camera projection (pinhole) | $u = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \quad v = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | $(X,Y,Z)$ world point → $(u,v)$ image point | px |
| 12 | Camera matrix | $\mathbf{x} = K [R \mid t] \mathbf{X}$ | $K$=intrinsic, $R,t$=extrinsic | homogeneous |
| 13 | Triangulation depth | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $B$=stereo baseline, $d$=disparity | mm |
| 14 | Circularity / Roundness | $C = \dfrac{4\pi A}{P^2}$ | $A$=area, $P$=perimeter | dimensionless |
| 15 | Centroid of a blob | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{N}, \quad \bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum y_i}{N}$ | $(x_i, y_i)$ = pixel coordinates of object | px |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Warning about Table Syntax** — In all markdown tables above, the absolute-value sign `|x|` has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` for safety. KTU students often lose presentation marks when tables fail to render. Do *not* use `|` inside any math cell of a markdown table.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Vision Task | Industrial Example |
|:-------------------|:------------|:-------------------|
| Electronics | Solder-paste inspection | SMT line AOI machines |
| Automotive | Engine block dimensional check | $100\%$ dimensional QA |
| Pharma | Pill counting, label verification | GMP-compliant packaging |
| Food | Foreign-object detection | X-ray + vision hybrid |
| Logistics | Parcel dimensioning | CubiScan systems |
| Robotics | Random bin-picking | 3D point-cloud + 6-DOF pose |
| Semiconductor | Wafer defect classification | EUV mask inspection |
| Medical | Cell counting, pathology slides | AI-assisted diagnosis |

---

## 2.5 The 2D vs 3D Machine Vision Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Compare 2D and 3D Machine Vision."* Memorise this structural decomposition:

**2D Vision** operates on a single projected intensity image. Output: 2D coordinates $(x, y)$, area, perimeter, intensity statistics.

**3D Vision** reconstructs spatial geometry. Output: 3D coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$, surface normals, point clouds, volumes.

| Aspect | 2D Vision | 3D Vision |
|:-------|:----------|:----------|
| Data type | Single image | Multiple images / point cloud |
| Depth info | No | Yes |
| Lighting | Standard | Structured / laser line / stereo |
| Algorithms | Edge detection, thresholding | Triangulation, ICP, photogrammetry |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Speed | Very fast (ms) | Moderate (100 ms – 1 s) |
| Use case | Presence check | Robot guidance |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Pinhole Camera Projection Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Establish the geometric relationship between a 3D world point and its 2D projection on the image sensor.

**Step 1 — Define the geometric setup.**
Consider a pinhole aperture of negligible size located at the camera centre $O$. A point $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z)$ in world coordinates lies on the *optical axis at distance* $Z$ from the aperture. The image plane lies at focal distance $f$ behind the aperture.

**Step 2 — Apply similar triangles.**
The light ray from $\mathbf{X}$ passing through $O$ intersects the image plane at $\mathbf{x} = (x, y)$. By similar triangles, the horizontal projection ratio equals the vertical one:

$$\frac{x}{f} = \frac{X}{Z}, \qquad \frac{y}{f} = \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain the image coordinates.**

$$x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}, \qquad y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to homogeneous coordinates.**
Augmenting each vector with a 1 gives the matrix form. Let the *intrinsic camera matrix* be:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} f & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $(c_x, c_y)$ is the principal point in pixel coordinates.

**Step 5 — Combine with extrinsic rotation-translation.**
The world-to-camera transform is $\mathbf{X}_c = R \mathbf{X} + \mathbf{t}$, hence the full projection is:

$$\mathbf{x} = K \cdot [R \mid \mathbf{t}] \cdot \mathbf{X}$$

This is the **pinhole camera projection equation** — the foundation of all photogrammetry and stereo vision.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stereo Triangulation for Depth Recovery

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the depth $Z$ of a point from two calibrated cameras (a stereo rig).

**Step 1 — Setup.**
Two identical cameras with focal length $f$ are mounted with their optical axes parallel and separated by a *baseline* $B$. The same 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects onto the left image at column $x_L$ and onto the right image at column $x_R$.

**Step 2 — Define disparity.**
The *disparity* is the horizontal pixel offset between the two projections:

$$d = x_L - x_R$$

**Step 3 — Apply similar triangles to the left and right cameras.**
For the left camera:

$$x_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + \frac{f \cdot B}{2Z}$$

For the right camera:

$$x_R = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - \frac{f \cdot B}{2Z}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract to eliminate $X$.**

$$d = x_L - x_R = \frac{f \cdot B}{Z}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $Z$.**

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

**Engineering insight:** As disparity $d \to 0$, $Z \to \infty$. Thus, *distant objects have small disparity* and depth recovery degrades. This is the **fundamental limitation of stereo vision**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Otsu's Threshold Selection Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Find the optimal global threshold $t^*$ that separates foreground and background in a grayscale histogram.

**Step 1 — Normalise the histogram.**
Let $p_i$ be the normalised probability of intensity level $i$, for $i = 0, 1, \dots, 255$, so that $\sum p_i = 1$.

**Step 2 — Define class weights.**
A threshold $t$ splits intensities into two classes:

$$w_0(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{t} p_i, \qquad w_1(t) = \sum_{i=t+1}^{255} p_i$$

**Step 3 — Compute class means.**

$$\mu_0(t) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{t} i \cdot p_i}{w_0(t)}, \qquad \mu_1(t) = \frac{\sum_{i=t+1}^{255} i \cdot p_i}{w_1(t)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute within-class variance.**

$$\sigma_w^2(t) = w_0 \cdot \sigma_0^2 + w_1 \cdot \sigma_1^2$$

**Step 5 — Maximise the inter-class variance (equivalent to minimising $\sigma_w^2$).**

$$\sigma_b^2(t) = w_0 w_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$$

**Step 6 — The optimal threshold is:**

$$t^* = \arg\max_{t} \sigma_b^2(t)$$

**Engineering insight:** Otsu's method is *unsupervised* — it does not need labelled training data. It is widely used in KTU lab work because it works robustly on bimodal histograms (i.e., dark object on light background).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Algorithm Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this matrix to score full marks in any "compare two segmentation methods" question.

| Criterion | Global Thresholding | Edge Detection (Canny) | Region Growing | Template Matching |
|:----------|:-------------------|:-----------------------|:---------------|:------------------|
| Computational cost | Very low | Moderate | High | High (scales with template) |
| Robustness to noise | Low | High (Gaussian smoothing step) | Moderate | Moderate |
| Need for training data | No | No | No | No (template only) |
| Handles occlusion | Yes (if silhouette intact) | Partial | Yes | No |
| Handles rotation | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (unless multi-angle template bank) |
| Sub-pixel accuracy | No | Yes (with sub-pixel refinement) | Yes | No |
| Typical KTU application | O-ring presence | Crack detection on metal | PCB defect grouping | Logo verification |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Measurement Accuracy

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU numerical frequently tested:

**Problem.** A machine vision system must measure the diameter of a bolt. The FOV is 40 mm, the camera has 2048 horizontal pixels. Find:
1. The pixel resolution in mm/px.
2. The expected measurement accuracy using a sub-pixel factor $k = 0.25$.

**Solution.**

**Step 1 — Pixel resolution:**

$$R_{px} = \frac{FOV}{N_{pixels}} = \frac{40 \, \text{mm}}{2048 \, \text{px}}$$

$$R_{px} = 0.01953 \, \text{mm/px}$$

**Step 2 — Measurement accuracy:**

$$\delta = \pm R_{px} \cdot k = \pm 0.01953 \times 0.25$$

$$\delta = \pm 0.00488 \, \text{mm} \approx \pm 4.88 \, \mu m$$

**Interpretation:** Sub-pixel interpolation improves accuracy by 4× compared to integer-pixel quantisation.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 4 — Centroid and Area for Pick-and-Place

> [!NOTE]
> For a binary segmented object, the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ gives the pick-point, and the area $A$ gives the count of pixels covered.

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{A} \sum_{i=1}^{A} x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{A} \sum_{i=1}^{A} y_i, \quad A = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{px}} b_i$$

where $b_i = 1$ if pixel $i$ belongs to the foreground, else $0$.

**Orientation estimation (for gripper alignment):**

$$\theta = \frac{1}{2} \arctan\left( \frac{2 \mu_{1,1}}{\mu_{2,0} - \mu_{0,2}} \right)$$

where $\mu_{p,q}$ are central image moments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Block Diagram of a Machine Vision System

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical 7-block functional architecture** that KTU expects in the answer script for any 14-mark question on "Explain Machine Vision with a block diagram."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Illumination Source] --> B[Part Under Inspection]
    B --> C[Optics Lens]
    C --> D[Image Sensor CCD CMOS]
    D --> E[Frame Grabber Digitiser]
    E --> F[Image Processing Unit CPU GPU DSP]
    F --> G[Decision Logic Classifier]
    G --> H[Output Interface PLC Robot SCADA]
    H --> I[Actuator Conveyor Rejector]
    I --> B

    J[Human Machine Interface HMI] --> F
    F --> J
```

**Block-by-block narration (for answer script):**

1. **Illumination Source** → provides controlled photons onto the part.
2. **Part Under Inspection** → reflects/transmits light depending on its geometry.
3. **Optics (Lens)** → focuses the reflected light onto the sensor.
4. **Image Sensor** → converts photons into a digital image (CCD or CMOS).
5. **Frame Grabber** → digitises and transfers frames to the processor.
6. **Image Processing Unit** → executes filtering, segmentation, feature extraction.
7. **Decision Logic** → classifies the part as OK / Reject based on rules.
8. **Output Interface** → sends discrete/serial/networked signals to PLC.
9. **Actuator** → physically diverts OK or defective parts.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Image Processing Pipeline (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Raw Image Acquisition] --> P2[Pre-processing Filtering]
    P2 --> P3[Image Enhancement Contrast Histogram]
    P3 --> P4[Segmentation Thresholding Edge Region]
    P4 --> P5[Feature Extraction Blob Edge Corner Texture]
    P5 --> P6[Classification Rule based ML CNN]
    P6 --> P7[Decision Output OK Reject]
    P7 --> P8[Communication to PLC Robot]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — 2D vs 3D Vision System Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[2D Machine Vision]
        D1[Single Camera] --> D2[Flat Intensity Image]
        D2 --> D3[Algorithms Thresholding Template Matching]
        D3 --> D4[Output x y area intensity]
    end

    subgraph S2[3D Machine Vision]
        E1[Stereo Camera OR Laser Line Scanner OR ToF Sensor] --> E2[Point Cloud]
        E2 --> E3[Algorithms Triangulation ICP Photogrammetry]
        E3 --> E4[Output X Y Z pose normal]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Stereo Vision Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Left Camera] -->|optical axis| LC[Left Image Plane]
    R[Right Camera] -->|optical axis| RC[Right Image Plane]
    P[World Point P] --> L
    P --> R
    B[Baseline B] -.connects.-> L
    B -.connects.-> R
    D[Disparity d equals xL minus xR] -.computed from.-> LC
    D -.computed from.-> RC
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Vision-Guided Robotic Pick-and-Place Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conveyor Infeed] --> B[Vision Station]
    B --> C[6 Axis Robot]
    C --> D[Place Station OK]
    C --> E[Reject Bin]
    F[PLC Controller] --> B
    F --> C
    G[HMI Panel] --> F
    B --> F
    C --> F
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Lighting Strategies Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the inspection task?}
    Q1 -->|Dimensional measurement| A1[Backlight for silhouette]
    Q1 -->|Surface defect detection| A2[Diffuse dome light]
    Q1 -->|Specular reflective part| A3[Polarised ring light]
    Q1 -->|3D profile| A4[Structured light laser line]
    Q1 -->|High speed motion| A5[Strobed LED pulsed]
```

---

## 4.7 Diagram 7 — Coordinate Frames in a Vision System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[World Frame W] -->|R t| B[Camera Frame C]
    B -->|K intrinsic| C[Image Frame pixel u v]
    C -->|distortion model| D[Corrected Image]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define Machine Vision. List any four industrial applications of Machine Vision.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Machine Vision is an engineering discipline that employs optical sensors, digital image processing, and intelligent algorithms to automatically acquire, analyse, and interpret visual information from a scene for industrial inspection, measurement, and control without human intervention.

**Four industrial applications:**
1. Automated optical inspection (AOI) of printed circuit boards in SMT lines.
2. Dimensional gauging of machined automotive components.
3. Bottle-fill-level inspection in pharmaceutical packaging.
4. Robotic bin-picking using 3D vision-guided pick-and-place.

> **[Examiner's Note — 3 marks distribution]**
> - 1 mark for *correct formal definition*.
> - 2 marks for any four valid applications (0.5 mark each).

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between 2D and 3D Machine Vision with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 2D Machine Vision | 3D Machine Vision |
|:----------|:-----------------|:------------------|
| Output | 2D coordinates $(x,y)$, area | 3D coordinates $(X,Y,Z)$, pose |
| Sensors | Single area-scan camera | Stereo camera, laser line scanner, ToF |
| Algorithms | Thresholding, edge detection, template matching | Triangulation, ICP, photogrammetry |
| Depth info | Not available | Available |
| Example | O-ring presence check | Robot-guided bin-picking |
| Cost | Low | High |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Adapted Module 4, 14 Marks]`
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an industrial Machine Vision system. Discuss the role of each block.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A vision system uses a camera with focal length $f = 16$ mm placed at a working distance of $200$ mm. The sensor is $1/3$ inch format (sensor width $= 4.8$ mm) with $640$ horizontal pixels. Calculate (i) magnification, (ii) field of view, (iii) pixel resolution, (iv) measurement accuracy using sub-pixel factor $k = 0.25$.

---

### Model Answer — Question A(a)

**Block Diagram:**
*(Refer to SECTION 4.1 above — same 7-block functional architecture.)*

**Explanation of each block:**

1. **Illumination Source** — Provides controlled, uniform, and repeatable light on the part. Choice depends on surface type: backlighting for dimensional gauging, ring light for general purpose, structured light for 3D.

2. **Optics (Lens)** — Focuses the reflected light onto the image sensor. Determines magnification $M = s_i / s_o$ and depth of field.

3. **Image Sensor (CCD/CMOS)** — Converts the focused light into a discrete 2D array of pixel intensity values. CCD gives higher uniformity; CMOS gives higher speed and lower power.

4. **Frame Grabber** — Synchronises and digitises the analog sensor output into a digital image buffer.

5. **Image Processing Unit** — Executes multi-stage algorithms: pre-processing → segmentation → feature extraction → classification.

6. **Decision / I/O Unit** — Applies pass/fail rules and produces control signals.

7. **Communication Interface** — Transmits the decision to the PLC, robot, or SCADA over digital I/O, fieldbus, or industrial Ethernet.

> *(Full marks = labelled diagram (3) + role of each block (4) — 7 marks total)*

---

### Model Answer — Question A(b)

**Given data:**
- $f = 16$ mm
- Working distance (object distance) $s_o = 200$ mm
- Sensor width $W_{sensor} = 4.8$ mm
- Number of horizontal pixels $N_{px} = 640$
- Sub-pixel factor $k = 0.25$

**(i) Magnification:**

$$M = \frac{f}{s_o - f} = \frac{16}{200 - 16} = \frac{16}{184}$$

$$M \approx 0.0869$$

**(ii) Field of view (along sensor width):**

$$FOV = \frac{W_{sensor}}{M} = \frac{4.8}{0.0869}$$

$$FOV \approx 55.24 \, \text{mm}$$

**(iii) Pixel resolution:**

$$R_{px} = \frac{FOV}{N_{px}} = \frac{55.24}{640}$$

$$R_{px} \approx 0.0863 \, \text{mm/px}$$

**(iv) Measurement accuracy:**

$$\delta = \pm R_{px} \cdot k = \pm 0.0863 \times 0.25$$

$$\delta = \pm 0.0216 \, \text{mm} \approx \pm 21.6 \, \mu m$$

> *(Each sub-part 1.75 marks = 7 marks)*

---

> **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted Module 4, 14 Marks]` **(Alternative Choice)**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various lighting techniques used in machine vision with suitable diagrams. State how lighting choice affects inspection reliability.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Two stereo cameras with focal length $f = 12$ mm are mounted with a baseline $B = 80$ mm. A point is observed at disparity $d = 6$ pixels on a sensor with pixel pitch $p = 5 \, \mu m$. Compute (i) effective focal length in pixels, (ii) depth of the point, (iii) the disparity if the same point were at twice the depth, (iv) comment on the relationship between depth and disparity.

---

### Model Answer — Question B(a)

**Lighting techniques in machine vision:**

1. **Backlighting** — Light source placed behind the part. Produces a clean silhouette, ideal for *dimensional gauging* and *edge detection*. Hides surface marks.
2. **Diffuse Dome Lighting** — Hemispherical, uniform illumination. Reduces specular highlights on curved metallic surfaces. Used for *reflective parts* and *defect detection*.
3. **Ring Lighting** — Coaxial or oblique ring of LEDs around the lens. General-purpose, reduces shadows, good for *PCB inspection*.
4. **Structured / Laser Line Lighting** — Projects a line or grid onto the part. Used for *3D profiling* via triangulation.
5. **Strobe / Pulsed LED** — Short-duration high-intensity pulse to *freeze* high-speed motion on conveyor belts.
6. **Polarised Lighting** — Cross-polarisation suppresses glare from shiny surfaces.

**Effect on inspection reliability:**
Lighting is the *single largest contributor* to image quality. Inadequate lighting leads to:
- Inconsistent edge strength
- Specular glare (false defects)
- Low contrast (failure of thresholding)
- Variable results between day and night shifts

A robust vision system uses **diffuse, controlled, and repeatable** lighting — making the system insensitive to ambient conditions.

> *(Lighting techniques 4 marks + effect on reliability 3 marks = 7 marks)*

---

### Model Answer — Question B(b)

**Given:**
- $f = 12$ mm
- Baseline $B = 80$ mm
- Disparity $d = 6$ pixels
- Pixel pitch $p = 5 \, \mu m = 0.005$ mm

**(i) Effective focal length in pixels:**

$$f_{px} = \frac{f}{p} = \frac{12}{0.005} = 2400 \, \text{pixels}$$

**(ii) Depth of the point:**

$$Z = \frac{f_{px} \cdot B}{d} = \frac{2400 \times 80}{6}$$

$$Z = 32000 \, \text{mm} = 32 \, \text{m}$$

**(iii) Disparity at twice the depth ($Z' = 2Z = 64$ m):**

Since $Z \cdot d = f_{px} \cdot B$ is constant,

$$d' = \frac{f_{px} \cdot B}{Z'} = \frac{f_{px} \cdot B}{2Z} = \frac{d}{2}$$

$$d' = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \, \text{pixels}$$

**(iv) Relationship between depth and disparity:**
Disparity is *inversely proportional* to depth. As $Z$ doubles, $d$ halves. This non-linear relation is the reason why *stereo vision loses precision at long range*, and why structured light is preferred for short-range 3D scanning.

> *(Each sub-part 1.75 marks = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing object distance with image distance** in the thin-lens equation. Always remember: $s_o$ is in front of the lens, $s_i$ is behind it.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mm to pixels** in focal-length calculations. The cleanest approach: first convert $f$ and $B$ into pixel units, *then* use the disparity formula.
> 3. **Using `|` (absolute value) inside markdown tables** — breaks the table render. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
> 4. **Drawing only a camera in the block diagram** — KTU expects the *complete pipeline*: lighting → optics → sensor → processor → decision → output.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** Always append the unit. A correct number without a unit is **0.5 mark** less in KTU valuation.
> 6. **Writing machine vision algorithms without justifying lighting choice.** KTU loves questions where lighting, sensor, and algorithm are *co-designed*. Always state why a particular lighting was chosen.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density bullet summary — final read before entering the exam hall.**

- **Machine Vision = non-contact optical sensing + image processing + automated decision.**
- **Core pipeline (KTU-mandated):** Lighting → Optics → Sensor → Frame Grabber → Processing → Decision → Output.
- **Camera equation (pinhole):** $x = f \cdot X / Z$.
- **Camera matrix (full):** $\mathbf{x} = K [R \mid t] \mathbf{X}$.
- **Pixel resolution:** $R_{px} = FOV / N_{pixels}$ in **mm/px**.
- **Measurement accuracy:** $\delta = \pm R_{px} \cdot k$ where $k$ is sub-pixel factor (typically 0.1–0.25).
- **Stereo depth:** $Z = f \cdot B / d$ — disparity is *inversely proportional* to depth.
- **Otsu's threshold:** $t^* = \arg\max_t \sigma_b^2(t) = w_0 w_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$.
- **Sobel gradient magnitude:** $\nabla I = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$.
- **Hough transform parameterisation:** $\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$.
- **Circularity:** $C = 4\pi A / P^2$ — value 1 means perfect circle.
- **Centroid:** $(\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = \left( \sum x_i / N, \sum y_i / N \right)$.
- **2D vs 3D:** 2D outputs $(x,y)$; 3D outputs $(X,Y,Z)$ + pose.
- **Sensor types:** CCD (high uniformity) vs CMOS (high speed, low power).
- **Lighting rule of thumb:** Backlight for dimensions, dome for shiny parts, ring for general, structured light for 3D, strobe for high-speed.
- **Outputs go to:** PLC (digital I/O), Robot (EtherCAT/PROFINET), SCADA (OPC-UA/MQTT).
- **Industrial protocols:** Digital I/O, EtherNet/IP, PROFINET, EtherCAT, TCP/IP.
- **Always state units in numerical answers.**
- **Always draw a labelled block diagram when asked to "explain" a vision system.**
- **Always justify the lighting choice.**
- **Always mention the specific algorithm used (Canny, Otsu, Hough, Sobel, etc.) when describing any processing step.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Acquisition and Digitizing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT633 - ADVANCED AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Image Acquisition and Digitizing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Image Acquisition** is the foundational first stage of any machine vision or automated inspection system, in which real-world visual information from a physical scene is optically captured by a sensor and converted into a numerical, discrete representation that a digital computer can process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> *Image acquisition is the process of capturing an optical image of a real-world object/scene and converting it into a discrete two-dimensional numerical array (matrix of pixel intensities) through the combined processes of sampling (spatial discretization) and quantization (intensity discretization).*

The complete acquisition chain consists of three sub-processes:

1. **Optical Projection** – Lenses focus light from the 3-D scene onto a 2-D sensing plane.
2. **Sensing / Transduction** – Photon energy is converted to an analog electrical signal (electrons).
3. **Digitization (A/D Conversion)** – The analog signal is sampled spatially (sampling) and quantized in intensity (quantization) to yield a digital image $f(x, y)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Anchors (must memorize):**
> - **Pixel (Picture Element):** The smallest addressable unit of a digital image.
> - **Sampling:** Spatial discretization → determines image size $(M \times N)$.
> - **Quantization:** Intensity discretization → determines gray-level resolution ($k$ bits/pixel).
> - **Resolution:** Combined measure of spatial detail + intensity detail.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a classroom and want to *describe* a painting on the wall to a friend who is **blind and living in another city**, using only a *telephone*.

- You cannot send the painting itself.
- Instead, you place a **transparent grid (chessboard)** over the painting.
- For each square of the grid, you read out the **average brightness** (say, a number from 0 = black to 255 = white).
- You then transmit a long list of numbers over the phone.

Your friend reconstructs the painting by **filling in each square with the number you read**. 

> **The grid is sampling. The brightness number is quantization. The full list is the digital image.**

In automation: the camera is your **eye**, the grid is the **sensor's pixel array**, the brightness number is the **analog-to-digital converter (ADC)**, and the phone line is the **computer bus**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Bit depth of commercial cameras:** typically **8-bit** (256 levels) for inspection, **10–12 bit** for metrology, **16-bit** for scientific imaging.
- **Standard image sizes:** **640 × 480** (VGA), **1920 × 1080** (Full HD), **4096 × 3072** (12 MP).
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (relevant for sensor timing).
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s (governs photoelectric conversion in CCD/CMOS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy of a single photon** of wavelength $\lambda$ is given by:  
> $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ — this sets the fundamental physical limit on low-light imaging.

---

## 1.4 Components of an Image Acquisition System (Block View)

A typical industrial vision system contains these hardware blocks, each of which is a **possible exam question**:

| # | Block | Function | KTU-Exam-Relevant Term |
|---|-------|----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Illumination** | Controls scene radiance | Backlight, ring light, structured light |
| 2 | **Lens** | Projects scene onto sensor | Focal length $f$, F-number $N$ |
| 3 | **Sensor** | Photon → electron | CCD or CMOS |
| 4 | **A/D Converter** | Analog → Digital | Sampling rate, bit depth |
| 5 | **Frame Buffer** | Stores digital image | $M \times N \times k$ bits |
| 6 | **Processor** | Performs image processing | FPGA, DSP, CPU |

---

## 1.5 Types of Images Acquired in Automation

In KTU Module 4, you must recognize four canonical image types:

- **Binary Image:** $k = 1$ bit/pixel → values 0 or 1 (defect detection).
- **Grayscale Image:** typically $k = 8$ bit/pixel → 256 gray levels (most inspection tasks).
- **Color (RGB) Image:** $k = 24$ bit/pixel → 8 bits per channel × 3 channels.
- **Multispectral / Hyperspectral:** $k > 24$ bits, multiple narrow wavelength bands (food sorting, material classification).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Operational Sub-Steps of Digitization

### Step 1 — Optical Projection (Energy Focusing)

A lens of focal length $f$ projects a 3-D point $(X, Y, Z)$ from the scene to a 2-D point $(x, y)$ on the sensor plane.

The **Gaussian thin-lens equation** governs focus:

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}$$

where $u$ = object distance, $v$ = image distance, $f$ = focal length.

The **magnification** of the optical system is:

$$m = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{h_{i}}{h_{o}}$$

- $h_{i}$ = image height on sensor.
- $h_{o}$ = object height in the scene.

> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** You will be asked to size a lens for a given field-of-view (FOV) — that is a direct application of this formula.

The **Field of View (FOV)** is:

$$\text{FOV} = \frac{\text{Sensor Size}}{\text{Magnification}}$$

---

### Step 2 — Sensing (Photon → Electron Conversion)

Two dominant sensor technologies:

- **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device):** Charge is shifted pixel-by-pixel to a common output node. Higher uniformity, lower noise, **global shutter** capability → preferred in industrial metrology.
- **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide Semiconductor):** Each pixel has its own amplifier and ADC. **Rolling shutter** in cheap versions, lower power, faster readout → preferred in high-speed automation lines.

The **photoelectric conversion** follows:

$$Q_{e} = \eta \cdot N_{photon}$$

where $\eta$ = quantum efficiency (typically 0.3 – 0.7), $Q_{e}$ = electrons generated, $N_{photon}$ = incident photons.

> [!NOTE]
> The resulting **noise** in a digital image has three classical sources: **shot noise** (Poisson on photons), **dark noise** (thermal electrons), and **read noise** (amplifier noise). Their variances add in quadrature: $\sigma^{2}_{total} = \sigma^{2}_{shot} + \sigma^{2}_{dark} + \sigma^{2}_{read}$.

---

### Step 3 — Digitization (Sampling + Quantization)

After the analog signal $V(t)$ is generated by the sensor, the A/D converter performs:

1. **Sampling (Spatial Discretization)** — The continuous 2-D image $f(x, y)$ is sampled on a uniform grid of $M \times N$ points. Sampling period $\Delta x$, $\Delta y$.
2. **Quantization (Intensity Discretization)** — Each continuous intensity value $f$ is mapped to one of $L = 2^{k}$ discrete gray levels, where $k$ = number of bits per pixel.

The complete digital image is the matrix:

$$F = \begin{bmatrix} f(0,0) & f(0,1) & \cdots & f(0,N-1) \\ f(1,0) & f(1,1) & \cdots & f(1,N-1) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ f(M-1,0) & f(M-1,1) & \cdots & f(M-1,N-1) \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 2.2 Sampling Theorem (Nyquist Criterion) — *High-Yield KTU Topic*

To faithfully reconstruct a continuous image from its samples, the sampling frequency $f_{s}$ must be at least **twice the maximum spatial frequency** in the image:

$$f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} \quad \text{(Nyquist Rate)}$$

Equivalently, in terms of resolution:

$$\text{Object Resolution} \leq 2 \cdot \text{Pixel Size (on object plane)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> If the Nyquist criterion is violated, **aliasing** occurs — fine details appear as low-frequency moiré patterns. This is a *favourite 7-mark question* in KTU.

---

## 2.3 Quantization Analysis

The **quantization step size** $\Delta$ for a signal with dynamic range $[f_{min}, f_{max}]$ and $L$ levels is:

$$\Delta = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{L - 1} \approx \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{2^{k}}$$

The **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** in decibels:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot k + 1.76 \;\; \text{dB}$$

- For **8-bit** image: SQNR ≈ **49.9 dB**.
- For **12-bit** image: SQNR ≈ **74.0 dB**.

> **Why this matters:** A 1-bit increase in $k$ improves SQNR by **~6 dB** — engineers use this to choose sensor bit depth.

---

## 2.4 Image Storage & Data Rate

The **total data size** of a raw grayscale image:

$$S = M \cdot N \cdot k \;\; \text{bits} = \frac{M \cdot N \cdot k}{8} \;\; \text{bytes}$$

The **data rate** for a camera running at $R$ frames per second (fps):

$$D = M \cdot N \cdot k \cdot R \;\; \text{bits/s}$$

> **Example (KTU-style):** A **1024 × 1024**, 8-bit grayscale camera at 30 fps:  
> $D = 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 \times 30 = 251{,}658{,}240$ bps $\approx$ **240 Mbps**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Thin lens equation | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{u} + \dfrac{1}{v}$ | All distances in mm |
| 2 | Magnification | $m = v/u = h_{i}/h_{o}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Field of View | $\text{FOV} = \text{Sensor Size}/m$ | mm |
| 4 | Nyquist Sampling | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Cycles/pixel |
| 5 | Quantization step | $\Delta = (f_{max} - f_{min})/(2^{k} - 1)$ | Intensity units |
| 6 | SQNR | $6.02k + 1.76$ | dB |
| 7 | Image size | $S = M \cdot N \cdot k$ | bits |
| 8 | Data rate | $D = M \cdot N \cdot k \cdot R$ | bps |
| 9 | Number of gray levels | $L = 2^{k}$ | Levels |
| 10 | Image histogram sum | $\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} h(i) = M \cdot N$ | Pixels |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive welding inspection:** 5 MP camera at 30 fps identifies micro-cracks; bit depth $k = 10$ is essential.
- **Pharmaceutical pill counting:** Binary threshold on a backlit image → 1-bit quantization.
- **PCB AOI (Automated Optical Inspection):** 12-bit sensor with telecentric lens to detect 25 µm track defects.
- **Robotic pick-and-place:** Structured-light 3-D acquisition (a 2-D image is the starting point).

> [!NOTE]
> In **structured-light scanning**, a known pattern is projected; the *digitized image* of the deformed pattern is the input to triangulation algorithms that yield 3-D coordinates. This is a frequent application-context question in KTU.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Number of Pixels Required (Resolution Sizing Problem)

> **Problem Statement (frequent KTU Part-B style):**  
> *"An industrial inspection system must detect a defect of minimum size 0.1 mm. The lens magnification is 0.5× and the sensor pixel pitch is 5 µm. Determine whether the system can resolve the defect. If not, what magnification is needed?"*

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the size of the defect on the sensor plane.**

The image size on the sensor is the object size multiplied by magnification $m$:

$$h_{i} = m \cdot h_{o} = 0.5 \times 0.1 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ mm} = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of sensor pixels that span this image size.**

The number of pixels covering a feature of size $h_i$ is:

$$n_{px} = \frac{h_{i}}{p} = \frac{50 \,\mu\text{m}}{5 \,\mu\text{m}} = 10 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Nyquist criterion.**

To *reliably resolve* a feature (not merely detect its presence), the Nyquist criterion requires that the feature span at least **2 pixels** on the sensor. The defect is currently imaged across 10 pixels, so the criterion is *easily satisfied*. ✅

> **Conclusion:** The current system is **over-resolved** — 5 pixels per feature is the typical engineering minimum, so the system can resolve the defect.

**Step 4 — (Follow-up) What magnification is needed to image 0.1 mm onto exactly 2 pixels?**

We require $h_i = 2 \cdot p = 2 \times 5 \,\mu\text{m} = 10 \,\mu\text{m} = 0.01$ mm.

$$m_{min} = \frac{h_{i}}{h_{o}} = \frac{0.01 \text{ mm}}{0.1 \text{ mm}} = 0.1 \times$$

So the lens magnification must be **at least $0.1 \times$** to meet Nyquist — anything lower and the defect would alias.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Storage and Data Rate for a Vision System

> **Problem Statement:**  
> *"A color (RGB) inspection camera has a resolution of $2048 \times 1536$ pixels and runs at 25 frames per second with 8-bit quantization per channel. Calculate (a) the total memory required to store one frame, (b) the data rate in Mbps, (c) the SQNR in dB, and (d) the minimum hard-disk capacity to record 1 hour of footage."*

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Set up the parameters.**

$$M = 2048, \quad N = 1536, \quad R = 25 \text{ fps}, \quad k_{\text{channel}} = 8 \text{ bits}, \quad n_{ch} = 3$$

Total bits per pixel: $k = n_{ch} \cdot k_{\text{channel}} = 3 \times 8 = 24$ bits.

**Step 2 — (a) Memory per frame.**

$$S = M \cdot N \cdot k = 2048 \times 1536 \times 24$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$S = 2048 \times 1536 \times 24 = 2048 \times 36864 = 75{,}497{,}472 \text{ bits}$$

Convert to megabytes (divide by $8 \times 2^{20}$):

$$S_{MB} = \frac{75{,}497{,}472}{8 \times 1{,}048{,}576} = \frac{75{,}497{,}472}{8{,}388{,}608} = 9.0 \text{ MB per frame}$$

**Step 3 — (b) Data rate in Mbps.**

$$D = M \cdot N \cdot k \cdot R = 75{,}497{,}472 \times 25 = 1{,}887{,}436{,}800 \text{ bps}$$

Converting to Mbps (divide by $10^6$):

$$D_{Mbps} = \frac{1{,}887{,}436{,}800}{1{,}000{,}000} = 1887.4 \text{ Mbps} \approx 1.89 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 4 — (c) SQNR in dB (per channel).**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot k_{\text{channel}} + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — (d) Disk storage for 1 hour.**

Total frames in 1 hour:

$$N_{frames} = R \times 3600 = 25 \times 3600 = 90{,}000 \text{ frames}$$

Total bytes:

$$S_{total} = 9.0 \text{ MB} \times 90{,}000 = 810{,}000 \text{ MB} = 810 \text{ GB}$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{S_{total} \approx 810 \text{ GB per hour uncompressed}}$ — this is why **video compression (H.264/H.265)** is essential in machine-vision deployments.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Quantization Step and Bit Depth Selection

> **Problem Statement:**  
> *"A grayscale camera observes a part whose reflected intensity varies from $f_{min} = 20$ (darkest region) to $f_{max} = 220$ (brightest region). The system must distinguish intensity differences of at least 1 unit. (a) What is the minimum number of quantization bits required? (b) What is the resulting quantization step $\Delta$?"*

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Required number of gray levels.**

The dynamic range is:

$$f_{max} - f_{min} = 220 - 20 = 200 \text{ units}$$

For the system to distinguish intensity differences of 1 unit, the quantization step must be:

$$\Delta \leq 1 \text{ unit}$$

The relationship between $\Delta$ and $L$ is:

$$\Delta = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{L - 1} \leq 1$$

Solving for $L$:

$$L - 1 \geq f_{max} - f_{min} = 200 \implies L \geq 201$$

**Step 2 — Convert $L$ to bits $k$.**

We need $L = 2^{k} \geq 201$. Test values:

$$2^{7} = 128 < 201, \quad 2^{8} = 256 \geq 201$$

Therefore, the minimum bit depth is:

$$\boxed{k = 8 \text{ bits}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the actual quantization step.**

$$\Delta = \frac{220 - 20}{256 - 1} = \frac{200}{255} \approx 0.784 \text{ intensity units}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the SQNR.**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** Going to 9 bits would only marginally improve $\Delta$ (to $200/511 \approx 0.39$) but doubles storage — a classic cost/benefit trade-off.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Lens Selection for a Given FOV

> **Problem Statement:**  
> *"You must inspect a region $100 \text{ mm} \times 80 \text{ mm}$ on a workpiece using a 1/3-inch sensor (active area $4.8 \text{ mm} \times 3.6 \text{ mm}$). The working distance (object distance) is $u = 300$ mm. Determine the required focal length $f$."*

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute required magnification.**

$$m = \frac{\text{Sensor Size (horizontal)}}{\text{FOV (horizontal)}} = \frac{4.8 \text{ mm}}{100 \text{ mm}} = 0.048$$

**Step 2 — Compute image distance $v$ using $m = v/u$.**

$$v = m \cdot u = 0.048 \times 300 = 14.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply thin-lens equation.**

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{300} + \frac{1}{14.4}$$

Converting to a common denominator:

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{14.4 + 300}{300 \times 14.4} = \frac{314.4}{4320} = 0.07278 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

Therefore:

$$f = \frac{1}{0.07278} = 13.74 \text{ mm} \approx 14 \text{ mm}$$

> **Result:** A 14 mm focal-length lens is required. Always round **down** in KTU calculations to ensure the FOV is fully covered.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Camera Parameters

| Decision | Symbol | Rule of Thumb | KTU Trade-off Question |
|---------|--------|---------------|------------------------|
| Spatial resolution | $M \times N$ | Smallest feature $\geq 5$ pixels | More pixels → more data |
| Bit depth | $k$ | $L = 2^{k} \geq \text{Dynamic Range}$ | More bits → larger files |
| Frame rate | $R$ | $\geq 2 \times$ line speed / part length | Higher $R$ → need faster interface (GigE, CoaXPress, USB3) |
| Lens magnification | $m$ | $m = \text{Sensor size} / \text{FOV}$ | Low $m$ → large FOV but small features |
| Illumination | — | Backlight for silhouette, ring-light for surface | Affects contrast, not resolution |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Full Image Acquisition Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete functional flow from physical scene to digital image, with every internal signal labeled.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["3D PHYSICAL SCENE"] --> A2["ILLUMINATION SOURCE"]
    A2 --> A3["LENS OPTICS"]
    A3 --> A4["PHOTOSENSOR ARRAY CCD OR CMOS"]
    A4 --> A5["CHARGE TO VOLTAGE CONVERSION"]
    A5 --> A6["ANALOG SIGNAL VXYT"]
    A6 --> A7["SAMPLING CIRCUIT"]
    A7 --> A8["QUANTIZATION ADC"]
    A8 --> A9["DIGITAL IMAGE F X Y MATRIX"]
    A9 --> A10["FRAME BUFFER MEMORY"]
    A10 --> A11["IMAGE PROCESSOR FPGA OR CPU"]
    A11 --> A12["INSPECTION OUTPUT PART OK OR NOT OK"]

    A7 -.-> S1["M X N PIXELS"]
    A8 -.-> S2["K BITS PER PIXEL"]
    A11 -.-> S3["FEATURE EXTRACTION AND CLASSIFICATION"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Digitization Sub-Process (Sampling + Quantization)

This diagram zooms into the **A/D stage** — the heart of "digitizing".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1["ANALOG IMAGE F CONTINUOUS"] --> B2["SAMPLING ON UNIFORM GRID DELTA X DELTA Y"]
    B2 --> B3["SAMPLED IMAGE F M N REAL VALUES"]
    B3 --> B4["QUANTIZER L EQUAL INTERVALS"]
    B4 --> B5["DIGITAL IMAGE F M N INTEGER VALUES"]
    B5 --> B6["STORAGE IN M X N X K BIT MATRIX"]

    B2 -.-> C1["NYQUIST FS GE 2 FMAX"]
    B4 -.-> C2["DELTA EQUALS FMAX MINUS FMIN DIVIDED BY 2K MINUS 1"]
    B5 -.-> C3["GRAY LEVEL RANGE ZERO TO 2K MINUS 1"]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Image Acquisition Decision Flow

This block diagram maps the **decision process** a vision-system engineer follows when sizing an acquisition system — directly tied to KTU Part-B 14-mark problem patterns.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1["DEFINE MINIMUM DEFECT SIZE D MIN"] --> D2["SELECT SENSOR PIXEL PITCH P"]
    D2 --> D3["COMPUTE REQUIRED MAGNIFICATION M MIN EQUALS 2P DIVIDED BY D MIN"]
    D3 --> D4["COMPUTE FIELD OF VIEW FOV"]
    D4 --> D5["SELECT WORKING DISTANCE U"]
    D5 --> D6["COMPUTE FOCAL LENGTH F USING LENS EQUATION"]
    D6 --> D7["COMPUTE FRAME RATE R FROM LINE SPEED"]
    D7 --> D8["COMPUTE DATA RATE D EQUALS M N K R"]
    D8 --> D9["SELECT INTERFACE GIGE OR USB3 OR COAXPRESS"]
    D9 --> D10["FINAL ACQUISITION SPECIFICATION"]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: CCD vs CMOS Comparison (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CCD["CCD SENSOR"]
        E1["PIXEL PHOTODIODE"] --> E2["CHARGE SHIFT REGISTER"]
        E2 --> E3["COMMON OUTPUT AMPLIFIER"]
        E3 --> E4["SINGLE ADC OFF CHIP"]
    end

    subgraph CMOS["CMOS SENSOR"]
        F1["PIXEL PHOTODIODE"] --> F2["IN PIXEL AMPLIFIER"]
        F2 --> F3["IN PIXEL ADC"]
        F3 --> F4["PARALLEL DIGITAL BUS"]
    end

    E4 --> G1["ANALOG OUTPUT LOW NOISE HIGH UNIFORMITY"]
    F4 --> G2["DIGITAL OUTPUT HIGH SPEED LOW POWER"]
```

---

## 4.5 Schematic: Sampling and Quantization Visualization (1-D)

A 1-D illustration of how a continuous signal becomes a discrete digital array — although this is best understood as a drawing, the equivalent **functional flow** is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["CONTINUOUS SIGNAL FX"] --> H2["SAMPLE AT FX0 FX1 FX2 FX3"]
    H2 --> H3["SAMPLE VALUES 0.2 0.7 0.5 0.9"]
    H3 --> H4["QUANTIZE TO 3 BIT 8 LEVELS"]
    H4 --> H5["DIGITAL VALUES 1 6 4 7"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Sampling decides *horizontal grid points*; quantization decides *vertical amplitude levels*. The two operations are mathematically independent.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define image digitization. List the two fundamental sub-processes involved in digitizing a continuous image.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Image digitization is the process of converting a continuous two-dimensional image $f(x, y)$ (defined over a continuous spatial domain and continuous intensity range) into a finite discrete set of numerical values that can be stored and processed by a digital computer.

The two fundamental sub-processes are:

1. **Sampling** — Discretization of the spatial coordinates $(x, y)$ onto a regular grid of $M \times N$ points, producing a sampled image $f(m, n)$. Sampling period $\Delta x, \Delta y$ controls spatial resolution.

2. **Quantization** — Discretization of the intensity (gray-level) values into a finite set of $L = 2^{k}$ discrete levels, where $k$ is the number of bits per pixel. The quantization step is $\Delta = (f_{max} - f_{min})/(2^{k} - 1)$.

Sampling and quantization together produce the digital image matrix $F = [f(m, n)]_{M \times N}$, with $f(m, n) \in \{0, 1, \dots, 2^{k}-1\}$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between sampling and quantization. State the Nyquist criterion for sampling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Sampling | Quantization |
|-----------|----------|--------------|
| **Domain** | Spatial (coordinates $x, y$) | Intensity (gray level $f$) |
| **Effect** | Discretizes position | Discretizes amplitude |
| **Result** | $M \times N$ pixel grid | $L = 2^{k}$ gray levels |
| **Limiting artifact** | Aliasing (if too coarse) | False contours / posterization (if too few levels) |
| **Mathematical control** | Sampling frequency $f_{s}$ | Bit depth $k$ |

**Nyquist Criterion:** A band-limited continuous image whose highest spatial frequency is $f_{max}$ must be sampled at a frequency $f_{s}$ satisfying:

$$f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

This minimum sampling rate is called the **Nyquist rate**. Sampling below this rate produces aliasing, in which high-frequency content is misrepresented as low-frequency artifacts (e.g., moiré patterns).

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Module-level internal choice. *Attempt any ONE full question from the pair.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Image Sizing and Digitization Math*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted Model QP]**

**(a)** An industrial vision system is to inspect a **120 mm × 90 mm** region of a printed circuit board for defects as small as **0.2 mm**. A monochrome camera with a **1/2-inch sensor (active area 6.4 mm × 4.8 mm)** and a pixel pitch of **3.45 µm** is available. The lens has focal length **8 mm**.

- (i) Compute the **required lens magnification** to resolve the 0.2 mm defect.
- (ii) Compute the **image distance $v$** and check whether the working distance is feasible.
- (iii) Determine the **number of pixels covering the defect on the sensor** and verify the Nyquist criterion.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the chosen magnification, the system uses **8-bit quantization** and runs at **15 fps**.

- (i) Compute the **image data rate** in Mbps.
- (ii) Calculate the **SQNR** in dB.
- (iii) If a 30-minute inspection is to be recorded losslessly, find the **storage in GB**.

**(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step (i) — Required magnification.**

By the Nyquist criterion, the defect must span at least **2 pixels** on the sensor:

$$h_{i,min} = 2 \cdot p = 2 \times 3.45 \,\mu\text{m} = 6.9 \,\mu\text{m} = 0.0069 \text{ mm}$$

$$m_{min} = \frac{h_{i,min}}{h_{o}} = \frac{0.0069 \text{ mm}}{0.2 \text{ mm}} = 0.0345$$

$$\boxed{m_{min} = 0.0345 \times}$$

**Step (ii) — Image distance $v$.**

For a working distance $u$, the magnification also equals $m = v/u$. Choosing a typical industrial working distance, say $u = 250$ mm:

$$v = m \cdot u = 0.0345 \times 250 = 8.625 \text{ mm}$$

This is feasible (much greater than focal length $f = 8$ mm). Verifying with the lens equation:

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{250} + \frac{1}{8.625} = 0.004 + 0.1159 = 0.1199$$

$$f = 8.34 \text{ mm} \approx 8 \text{ mm} \;\; ✅$$

**Step (iii) — Pixels covering the defect and Nyquist check.**

Image size of the defect on sensor:

$$h_i = m \cdot h_o = 0.0345 \times 0.2 = 0.0069 \text{ mm} = 6.9 \,\mu\text{m}$$

Pixels spanning the defect:

$$n_{px} = \frac{h_i}{p} = \frac{6.9 \,\mu\text{m}}{3.45 \,\mu\text{m}} = 2.0 \text{ pixels}$$

Nyquist criterion is **just met** (2 pixels). To get 5 pixels (engineering margin), use $m = 0.086$.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step (i) — Image resolution in pixels.**

With $m = 0.0345$, the FOV is:

$$\text{FOV} = \frac{\text{Sensor Size}}{m} = \frac{6.4 \text{ mm}}{0.0345} = 185.5 \text{ mm} \approx 186 \text{ mm}$$

But we need FOV = 120 mm, so the actual FOV is much larger than required — we may need to crop, or accept a 185.5 mm FOV (with 90 mm in the other dimension already fine: $4.8/0.0345 = 139$ mm — but our width is 120 mm, OK). Using the full sensor:

$$M = \frac{6.4 \text{ mm}}{3.45 \,\mu\text{m}} \approx 1855 \text{ pixels}, \quad N \approx 1391 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step (ii) — Data rate.**

$$D = M \cdot N \cdot k \cdot R = 1855 \times 1391 \times 8 \times 15$$

Computing step by step:

$$1855 \times 1391 = 2{,}580{,}305 \text{ pixels/frame}$$

$$2{,}580{,}305 \times 8 = 20{,}642{,}440 \text{ bits/frame}$$

$$20{,}642{,}440 \times 15 = 309{,}636{,}600 \text{ bps} = 309.6 \text{ Mbps}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 310 \text{ Mbps}}$$

**Step (iii) — SQNR.**

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$

**Step (iv) — Storage for 30 minutes.**

Total frames: $15 \times 30 \times 60 = 27{,}000$ frames.

Bytes per frame: $\frac{1855 \times 1391 \times 8}{8} = 2{,}580{,}305$ bytes $\approx 2.46$ MB.

Total storage:

$$S_{total} = 2.46 \text{ MB} \times 27{,}000 = 66{,}420 \text{ MB} \approx 64.86 \text{ GB}$$

$$\boxed{S_{total} \approx 65 \text{ GB}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Sensor Comparison and Nyquist Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted Model QP]**

**(a)** Compare **CCD** and **CMOS** image sensors with respect to:
- (i) Readout architecture
- (ii) Noise performance
- (iii) Power consumption
- (iv) Suitability for high-speed industrial automation.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine-vision system uses a lens with **focal length 16 mm** at a working distance of **320 mm**, imaging onto a sensor with pixel pitch **5.2 µm**. A grating with spatial frequency **40 line-pairs/mm** is placed at the object plane.

- (i) Compute the **magnification** and the **object-plane sampling period** $\Delta x_o$.
- (ii) Compute the **image-plane spatial frequency** of the grating.
- (iii) Verify whether the **Nyquist criterion** is satisfied. State what artifact will appear if it is not.

**(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step (i) — Readout architecture.**

- **CCD:** Each pixel accumulates charge; rows are sequentially shifted to a single output amplifier/ADC. Charge-domain transfer.
- **CMOS:** Each pixel contains its own amplifier and (often) ADC. Pixel-parallel readout. Voltage-domain.

**Step (ii) — Noise performance.**

- CCD: Lower read noise (single amplifier, well-characterized), but **higher dark current** due to charge transfer inefficiency.
- CMOS: Higher pixel-level noise (in-pixel amplifier mismatch, **Fixed-Pattern Noise (FPN)**), but improved in modern designs with Column-Parallel ADC.

**Step (iii) — Power consumption.**

- CCD: Higher power (requires multiple high-voltage clock phases for charge transfer).
- CMOS: Lower power, single 3.3 V supply typical; suitable for battery-powered embedded vision.

**Step (iv) — Suitability for high-speed automation.**

- **CCD preferred** in **metrology / scientific imaging** (low noise, global shutter, high uniformity).
- **CMOS preferred** in **high-speed automation** (rolling/global shutter, on-chip processing, windowed ROI readout, > 1000 fps possible).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step (i) — Magnification and object-plane sampling period.**

Using the thin-lens equation:

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} \implies \frac{1}{16} = \frac{1}{320} + \frac{1}{v}$$

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{16} - \frac{1}{320} = \frac{20 - 1}{320} = \frac{19}{320}$$

$$v = \frac{320}{19} = 16.84 \text{ mm}$$

Magnification:

$$m = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{16.84}{320} = 0.0526$$

Object-plane sampling period (size of one pixel projected onto the object):

$$\Delta x_o = \frac{p}{m} = \frac{5.2 \,\mu\text{m}}{0.0526} = 98.86 \,\mu\text{m} \approx 0.0989 \text{ mm}$$

**Step (ii) — Image-plane spatial frequency of the grating.**

The grating has a spatial frequency $f_{o} = 40$ lp/mm in the object plane. The image-plane spatial frequency (in line-pairs per mm) is:

$$f_{i} = f_{o} \cdot m = 40 \times 0.0526 = 2.105 \text{ lp/mm}$$

**Step (iii) — Nyquist verification.**

The Nyquist frequency of the sensor is:

$$f_{Nyquist} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot p} = \frac{1}{2 \times 5.2 \,\mu\text{m}} = \frac{1}{0.0104 \text{ mm}} = 96.15 \text{ lp/mm}$$

Compare with the image-plane frequency:

$$f_{i} = 2.105 \text{ lp/mm} \ll f_{Nyquist} = 96.15 \text{ lp/mm}$$

**Nyquist criterion is comfortably satisfied** ✅ — the grating will be sampled far above the Nyquist rate, and no aliasing will occur.

> **If the criterion were violated:** Moiré patterns / aliasing would appear — false low-frequency bands would form in the digitized image, and fine line detail would be lost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Always convert mm ↔ µm ↔ m. Many students lose 1–2 marks by writing $0.2$ mm when it should be $0.0002$ m in SI form.
> 2. **Forgetting the "-1" in $L-1$:** The denominator of $\Delta = (f_{max} - f_{min})/(2^{k} - 1)$ has $2^{k} - 1$, not $2^{k}$. A *very common* error.
> 3. **Confusing sampling rate with frame rate:** Sampling frequency $f_s$ is in *cycles/pixel* (spatial), frame rate $R$ is in *fps* (temporal). They are different quantities.
> 4. **Off-by-one in pixel count:** For "resolving" a feature, the rule is **2 pixels minimum** (Nyquist), but **5 pixels** for **reliable measurement** (engineering rule). Examiners reward this distinction.
> 5. **Mistaking bit depth:** A "24-bit color image" is 8 bits × **3 channels**, not 24 bits per channel. Total per pixel is still 24 bits, but the *per-channel* SQNR uses 8 bits.
> 6. **Not stating the Nyquist criterion explicitly** in a derivation question — KTU examiners will deduct at least 1 mark for this even if the final answer is correct.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (print this before KTU exam!)**

### ✅ Concepts
- [ ] Image acquisition = Optics + Sensing + Digitization.
- [ ] Digitization = **Sampling** (space) + **Quantization** (intensity).
- [ ] Sampling gives $M \times N$ pixels; quantization gives $L = 2^{k}$ levels.
- [ ] A digital image is a **2-D integer matrix** $F = [f(m, n)]$.

### ✅ Key Sensors
- [ ] **CCD** → low noise, global shutter, serial readout.
- [ ] **CMOS** → high speed, low power, per-pixel readout, on-chip ADC.

### ✅ Critical Formulas (must memorize)
- [ ] Thin lens: $1/f = 1/u + 1/v$
- [ ] Magnification: $m = v/u = h_i/h_o$
- [ ] FOV: $\text{FOV} = \text{Sensor Size}/m$
- [ ] Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$
- [ ] Quantization step: $\Delta = (f_{max} - f_{min})/(2^{k}-1)$
- [ ] SQNR: $6.02k + 1.76$ dB
- [ ] Data rate: $D = M \cdot N \cdot k \cdot R$ bps
- [ ] Image size: $S = M \cdot N \cdot k$ bits

### ✅ Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Don't confuse $k$ (bits per pixel) with $L$ (gray levels).
- [ ] Don't use $2^k$ in place of $(2^k - 1)$ in the quantization step formula.
- [ ] For color images, $k_{total} = 3 \times k_{channel}$.
- [ ] Always verify Nyquist criterion in sizing problems.
