### ✅ Real-World Anchors (use these to score "application" marks)
- [ ] PCB AOI, pharmaceutical inspection, robotic pick-and-place, weld seam inspection, food sorting, barcode reading, 3-D structured-light scanning.

### ✅ 1-Line Memory Aid
> *"A **sample** tells you **where** to look; a **quantization level** tells you **how bright** it is."*

---

**End of Module 4 — Image Acquisition and Digitizing Notes**  
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned*  
*All formulas, derivations, and problems are directly aligned with the KTU 2024 B.Tech ESE valuation key.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Vision Applications
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT633 - ADVANCED AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machine Vision Applications

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Machine Vision (MV)** is the engineering discipline that provides automated, image-based inspection, measurement, and analysis capabilities to industrial automation systems. As per the KTU PEMRT633 syllabus, Machine Vision Applications refer to the deployment of computer vision algorithms, optical sensors, illumination systems, and digital signal processing hardware to enable machines (chiefly industrial robots, AGVs, and CNC systems) to interpret visual data and execute control decisions without direct human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Bank — Memorize this verbatim:**
> *Machine vision is a non-contact, optical measurement and inspection methodology that converts captured image data into actionable control signals for automated decision-making in manufacturing and process environments.*

The fundamental signal chain in any machine vision application is:

$$
\text{Object} \longrightarrow \text{Optics} \longrightarrow \text{Image Sensor} \longrightarrow \text{ADC} \longrightarrow \text{Processor} \longrightarrow \text{Decision Output}
$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine teaching a **colour-blind quality inspector** to identify defects on a moving conveyor belt. The inspector cannot rely on subjective judgement — they need fixed, measurable rules: *"Reject the bottle if its cap height deviates more than 0.5 mm from the reference, OR if the label is rotated beyond 5°."* 

A **machine vision system** is exactly that — a digital inspector with:
- **Eyes** (CCD/CMOS camera)
- **Memory** (frame buffer / image storage)
- **Logic** (image processing algorithms)
- **Hands** (output to actuators, PLCs, robots)

### 1.3 Core Components of a Machine Vision System

| # | Component | Engineering Function |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Illumination** | Stabilizes and enhances image contrast |
| 2 | **Optics (Lens)** | Focuses object onto sensor with required magnification |
| 3 | **Image Sensor** | Converts photons → electrical charge (CCD/CMOS) |
| 4 | **Frame Grabber / Interface** | Digitizes and transfers image (Camera Link, GigE Vision, USB3 Vision) |
| 5 | **Vision Processor** | Runs thresholding, edge detection, pattern matching |
| 6 | **Communication I/O** | Sends Pass/Fail or coordinates to PLC/Robot |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus emphasizes *applications* rather than algorithm theory. Hence, students must focus on **where, why, and how** machine vision is deployed in industrial automation, not on deriving convolution kernels.

### 1.4 Classification of Machine Vision Applications

Machine Vision Applications can be broadly classified into **six functional categories** recognized by industry standards (AIA — Automated Imaging Association):

1. **Gauging / Metrology** — Dimensional measurement
2. **Inspection / Defect Detection** — Surface flaw identification
3. **Recognition** — Reading codes, characters, parts
4. **Guidance** — Robot path alignment
5. **Identification** — Object type classification
6. **Verification** — Assembly correctness check

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Steps of a Vision-Guided Application

Every machine vision application (regardless of industry) executes a **four-stage pipeline**. KTU examiners frequently test this pipeline.

**Step 1 — Image Acquisition (Pre-processing)**
- Trigger the camera (hardware or software trigger).
- Capture a 2D intensity matrix $I(x,y)$ of size $M \times N$.
- Apply noise filtering (Gaussian, median).

**Step 2 — Image Enhancement**
- Adjust contrast using histogram equalization.
- Convert to grayscale or isolate channels: $I_{gray} = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$.

**Step 3 — Feature Extraction**
- Thresholding: pixels above threshold $T$ become binary.

$$
I_{binary}(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1, & I(x,y) \geq T \\ 0, & I(x,y) < T \end{cases}
$$

- Edge detection (Sobel, Canny): gradient magnitude $\vert \nabla I \vert$.

**Step 4 — Decision / Actuation**
- Compare measured features with reference tolerances.
- Send **PASS / FAIL** or coordinate data via digital I/O, Ethernet/IP, or Profinet to the actuator (robot, diverter, reject valve).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize symbols, units, and meaning.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|------------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $G = \dfrac{SD}{FOV}$ | Sensor size to field of view relation (G = magnification) | unitless |
| 2 | $PMAG = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Pixel Size}}{\text{Object Resolution}}$ | Pixel-to-object resolution mapping | $\mu m/\mu m$ |
| 3 | $L = \dfrac{f \cdot (D - f)}{D}$ | Lens focal length for given working distance | mm |
| 4 | $WD = f \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{M}\right)$ | Working distance from object to lens | mm |
| 5 | $\vert \nabla I \vert = \sqrt{\left(\tfrac{\partial I}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{\partial I}{\partial y}\right)^2}$ | Image gradient magnitude (edge strength) | intensity/mm |
| 6 | $T_{otsu} = \arg\max_{T} \sigma_B^2(T)$ | Otsu threshold (maximizes inter-class variance) | intensity |
| 7 | $R_{xy} = \dfrac{1}{M} \sum \sum I(x,y) I(x+dx, y+dy)$ | Normalized cross-correlation (pattern matching) | unitless |
| 8 | $A = \pi \cdot r^2$ | Area of circular blob (pixel count conversion) | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 9 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{G_y}{G_x}\right)$ | Edge orientation angle (Sobel) | radians |
| 10 | $D_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{f_{line}} \cdot v_{conveyor}$ | Image acquisition cycle time for moving parts | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule (Markdown Engine):** In the table above, vertical bars $\vert \cdot \vert$ denote **absolute value / magnitude** (NOT table column separators). This is mandated by the KTU Engine output rules to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Benefit |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| Automotive | Weld seam inspection | 100% inspection; 0.05 mm precision |
| Pharma | Blister pack verification | Eliminates missing-tablet defect |
| F&B | Label orientation check | Reduces recall risk |
| Electronics | PCB solder paste inspection | Early defect detection |
| Logistics | Barcode + dimensioning | Throughput up to 10× manual |
| Medical | Blood cell counting | 95% accuracy vs 80% manual |
| Aerospace | Composite layup defect detection | Critical safety compliance |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Field of View (FOV) and Working Distance Relationship

**Problem Statement:** Given a camera with sensor width $S_w$, lens focal length $f$, and desired field of view $FOV$ covering the inspection area, derive the working distance $WD$.

**Given:**
- Sensor dimension $S_w$ = **8.47 mm** (typical 1/3" CCD)
- Focal length $f$ = **12 mm**
- Required FOV = **200 mm**

**Step 1 — State the lens magnification equation.**

$$
M = \frac{S_w}{FOV}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
M = \frac{8.47}{200} = 0.04235
$$

**Step 3 — Apply thin-lens formula relating magnification to object distance.**

$$
M = \frac{f}{WD - f}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Working Distance $WD$.**

$$
WD - f = \frac{f}{M}
$$

$$
WD = f + \frac{f}{M} = f \left( 1 + \frac{1}{M} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values.**

$$
WD = 12 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{0.04235} \right) = 12 \times 24.615
$$

$$
WD \approx 295.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The lens must be positioned approximately **295 mm** from the object for a 200 mm horizontal field of view. Any deviation alters magnification, causing measurement error.

### 3.2 Derivation: Otsu's Threshold (Inter-class Variance Maximization)

**Step 1 — Define image histogram probability.**

$$
p_i = \frac{n_i}{N}, \quad \sum_{i=0}^{L} p_i = 1
$$

where $n_i$ is the pixel count at intensity level $i$, $N$ is total pixel count, and $L$ = 255 (8-bit image).

**Step 2 — Define class probabilities for threshold $T$.**

$$
\omega_0(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{T} p_i, \quad \omega_1(T) = \sum_{i=T+1}^{L} p_i
$$

**Step 3 — Define class means.**

$$
\mu_0(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{T} \frac{i \, p_i}{\omega_0}, \quad \mu_1(T) = \sum_{i=T+1}^{L} \frac{i \, p_i}{\omega_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Global mean and between-class variance.**

$$
\mu_T = \omega_0 \mu_0 + \omega_1 \mu_1
$$

$$
\sigma_B^2(T) = \omega_0(\mu_0 - \mu_T)^2 + \omega_1(\mu_1 - \mu_T)^2
$$

**Step 5 — Optimization objective.**

$$
T_{otsu} = \arg\max_{0 \leq T < L} \sigma_B^2(T)
$$

**Physical Meaning:** $T_{otsu}$ is the intensity value that best separates **foreground (defect/object)** from **background (clean surface)** — making it the cornerstone of automated defect detection.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Vision Application vs Method

| Application Domain | Vision Technique | Sensor Type | Typical Resolution | Inspection Rate |
|--------------------|------------------|-------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Weld bead inspection | 2D + structured laser line | CMOS area scan | 0.1 mm | 30 parts/min |
| Bottle cap presence | 2D binary thresholding | CCD line scan | 0.5 mm | 600 parts/min |
| PCB solder paste | 3D SPI (Solder Paste Inspection) | Laser triangulation | 10 $\mu$m | 120,000 components/hr |
| Car body alignment | 3D stereo vision / Time-of-Flight | Stereo cameras | 0.2 mm | 60 jigs/hr |
| Pick-and-place robot | 2D pattern matching | CMOS global shutter | 0.05 mm | 90 cycles/min |
| Barcode reading | 1D/2D code symbology | CMOS rolling shutter | 0.2 mm | 1500 reads/min |
| Medical cell counting | Bright-field microscopy + CNN | High-resolution CMOS | 0.5 $\mu$m/pixel | 200 slides/hr |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Machine Vision Inspection Station

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Conveyor / Workpiece Infeed] --> B[Photoelectric Trigger Sensor]
    B --> C[Camera Acquisition Module]
    C --> D[Lens Optics Assembly]
    D --> E[LED Ring / Bar Illumination]
    C --> F[Frame Grabber / PoE Switch]
    F --> G[Vision Processor DSP / IPC]
    G --> H[Image Processing Pipeline]
    H --> I[Feature Extraction Engine]
    I --> J[Decision Logic PASS FAIL]
    J --> K[Digital I/O to PLC]
    K --> L[Pneumatic Reject Cylinder]
    J --> M[Ethernet IP to Robot Controller]
    M --> N[6 Axis Robot Pick and Place]
    J --> O[HMI Dashboard Display]
    O --> P[Operator Alert]
```

### 4.2 Application Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[Machine Vision Applications]
    Root --> Q1[Inspection]

    Root --> Q4[Metrology]

    Q1 --> Q1a[Surface Defect Detection]
    Q1 --> Q1b[Flaw and Crack Detection]
    Q1 --> Q1c[Assembly Verification]

    Q2 --> Q2a[Robot Pick and Place]
    Q2 --> Q2b[AGV Lane Following]
    Q2 --> Q2c[Welding Seam Tracking]

    Q3 --> Q3a[Barcode and QR Reading]
    Q3 --> Q3b[OCR Optical Character Recognition]
    Q3 --> Q3c[Part Type Recognition]

    Q4 --> Q4a[2D Dimensional Gauging]
    Q4 --> Q4b[3D Profilometry]
    Q4 --> Q4c[Color Inspection]
```

### 4.3 Vision-Guided Robot Pick-and-Place Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PLC as PLC Controller
    participant Cam as Vision Camera
    participant Proc as Vision Processor
    participant Robot as 6-Axis Robot
    participant Conv as Conveyor Belt

    Conv->>PLC: Part arrives at inspection zone
    PLC->>Cam: Hardware trigger pulse
    Cam->>Proc: Image data via GigE Vision
    Proc->>Proc: Threshold and blob analysis
    Proc->>Proc: Compute centroid (x_c, y_c, theta)
    Proc->>Robot: Transmit target coordinates
    Robot->>Robot: Inverse kinematics solve
    Robot->>Conv: Pick part
    Robot->>Robot: Place at designated slot
    Robot->>PLC: Cycle complete acknowledgment
```

### 4.4 Closed-Loop Quality Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference CAD Model] --> B[Vision System Setpoint]
    B --> C[Live Image Capture]
    C --> D[Feature Extraction]
    D --> E[Compare with Tolerance]
    E -->|Within Tolerance| F[PASS]
    E -->|Outside Tolerance| G[FAIL]
    G --> H[Reject Mechanism]
    F --> I[Continue Production]
    H --> J[SPC Database Logging]
    J --> K[Statistical Process Control]
    K --> L[Process Adjustment Feedback]
    L --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three major industrial applications of machine vision systems and explain one in detail.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three major industrial applications of machine vision systems are:

1. **Automated Inspection** — Detecting surface defects, cracks, scratches on manufactured parts at high speed using 2D image processing.
2. **Robotic Guidance** — Providing real-time coordinates to robots for accurate pick-and-place or welding operations.
3. **Identification and Tracking** — Reading barcodes, QR codes, and alphanumeric characters for traceability in logistics and packaging.

*Detailed explanation — Robotic Guidance:* A vision-guided robot uses a camera mounted on its end-effector (eye-in-hand) or fixed above the workspace (eye-to-hand). The image processor computes the **centroid coordinates** and **orientation angle** of the target object. These are transmitted to the robot controller, which performs inverse kinematics to align the gripper with the part regardless of conveyor position drift. This eliminates the need for mechanical jigs, enabling flexible manufacturing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the role of illumination in a machine vision system? Name two illumination techniques used.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of illumination:**
Illumination determines the **contrast, stability, and repeatability** of captured images. Proper lighting reduces shadows, eliminates specular glare, and enhances the features (edges, textures, defects) of interest. Poor illumination accounts for nearly **70% of failed vision deployments** in industry.

**Two illumination techniques:**

1. **Backlighting** — A uniform light source placed behind the object creates a silhouette, ideal for dimensional gauging and contour measurement.
2. **Structured Lighting** — A laser line or dot pattern is projected onto the object; distortion of the pattern reveals 3D surface profile (used in 3D scanning and weld inspection).

(Other valid answers: Ring lighting, diffuse dome lighting, coaxial illumination, dark-field illumination.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the functional architecture of an industrial machine vision system used for automated inspection on a production line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe three real-world applications of machine vision in the automotive industry, highlighting the type of inspection performed and the benefit achieved. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Functional Architecture of an Industrial Machine Vision Inspection System (7 marks):**

The block diagram is illustrated below:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Illumination Module] --> B[Object Under Inspection]
    B --> C[Lens Optics]
    C --> D[Image Sensor CCD CMOS]
    D --> E[Frame Grabber Digitizer]
    E --> F[Vision Processor]
    F --> G[Image Processing Algorithms]
    G --> H[Decision Logic PASS FAIL]
    H --> I[PLC / Robot Controller]
    I --> J[Actuator Reject or Accept]
```

**Working Principle:**

1. **Illumination Module** — Projects controlled light (LED ring, bar light, backlight) onto the object to maximize contrast.
2. **Object** — The component placed under inspection (e.g., bottle cap, PCB, weld seam).
3. **Lens Optics** — Focuses the reflected/transmitted light onto the image sensor with appropriate magnification.
4. **Image Sensor** — A CCD or CMOS array converts photons into electrical charge proportional to light intensity, producing a digital image matrix $I(x,y)$.
5. **Frame Grabber** — Synchronizes image capture with the conveyor trigger and digitizes the analog signal (now obsolete in modern smart cameras).
6. **Vision Processor** — Runs image processing algorithms: noise filtering, thresholding, edge detection, pattern matching.
7. **Decision Logic** — Compares extracted features against tolerance limits stored in the reference database.
8. **Output Interface** — Sends a **Pass/Fail signal** via digital I/O, Ethernet/IP, or PROFINET to the PLC, which triggers the reject cylinder or robot.

**Key Advantages:** Non-contact, 100% inspection, sub-millisecond cycle time, zero operator fatigue.

---

**(b) Three Real-World Machine Vision Applications in the Automotive Industry (7 marks):**

**1. Weld Seam Inspection (2 marks)**
- A 2D camera combined with **structured laser line** illumination captures the cross-sectional profile of MIG/MAG welds.
- The vision system measures **weld width, height, and undercut**.
- **Benefit:** Eliminates human radiographic testing; detects porosity, cracks, and insufficient penetration in real time.

**2. Robotic Bin Picking (2 marks)**
- A 3D vision system (stereo cameras or Time-of-Flight) identifies randomly oriented parts in a bin.
- The processor computes the **6-DOF pose** (x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw) of each part.
- **Benefit:** Enables flexible automation without fixturing; reduces part damage and cycle time.

**3. Surface Defect Detection on Car Body Panels (3 marks)**
- High-resolution line-scan cameras mounted on a gantry scan painted body panels.
- Defects such as dents, paint runs, orange peel, and dust inclusions are detected using **morphological image processing**.
- **Benefit:** Achieves 100% inspection quality; saves ₹ lakhs in warranty claims; reduces manual rework.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** With relevant diagrams, explain the role of machine vision in **robotic pick-and-place systems**. Discuss how image processing is used to identify object orientation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine vision camera has a sensor width of **8.8 mm** and uses a lens of focal length **16 mm** to inspect a rectangular workpiece of width **150 mm**. Calculate:
   (i) the magnification of the optical setup,
   (ii) the working distance,
   (iii) the smallest detectable feature if one pixel corresponds to **5.2 $\mu$m** on the sensor. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Machine Vision in Robotic Pick-and-Place (7 marks):**

In robotic pick-and-place systems, machine vision serves as the **localization and orientation engine** that enables the robot to pick randomly placed parts from a conveyor or bin.

**System Layout:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Conveyor with Random Parts] --> B[Overhead Vision Camera]
    B --> C[Vision Processor]
    C --> D[Image Segmentation]
    D --> E[Centroid and Angle Calculation]
    E --> F[Coordinate Transmission via Ethernet]
    F --> G[Robot Controller]
    G --> H[6 Axis Articulated Robot]
    H --> I[Pick the Part]
    I --> J[Place in Tray or Machine]
```

**Image Processing Steps to Identify Orientation:**

1. **Image Capture** — A trigger sensor activates the camera as the part enters the field of view.
2. **Pre-processing** — Gaussian filtering removes noise.
3. **Thresholding** — Pixels are converted to binary using Otsu's method to separate the part from the background.
4. **Blob Analysis** — Connected pixel regions are labeled; the **centroid** $(x_c, y_c)$ is computed:

$$
x_c = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \quad y_c = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i
$$

5. **Orientation Estimation** — The **minimum bounding rectangle (MBR)** is fitted around the blob. The angle $\theta$ of the MBR's major axis gives the **in-plane rotation** of the part.
6. **Pick Decision** — The robot gripper is aligned to $(x_c, y_c, \theta)$ and lowered to pick the part.

**Benefit:** Achieves **cycle times of 60–90 picks/minute**, eliminates mechanical jigs, and handles part variants via recipe selection.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Sensor width $S_w = 8.8$ mm
- Focal length $f = 16$ mm
- Workpiece width (FOV) = $150$ mm
- Pixel size = $5.2$ $\mu$m = $0.0052$ mm

---

**(i) Magnification $M$:**

$$
M = \frac{S_w}{FOV} = \frac{8.8}{150}
$$

$$
M = 0.0587 \quad (\text{unitless})
$$

**Marks: 2**

---

**(ii) Working Distance $WD$:**

$$
WD = f \left( 1 + \frac{1}{M} \right)
$$

$$
WD = 16 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{0.0587} \right) = 16 \times 18.04
$$

$$
WD \approx 288.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Marks: 2**

---

**(iii) Smallest Detectable Feature:**

The smallest feature resolvable equals one pixel mapped to the object plane.

$$
\text{Object resolution} = \frac{\text{Pixel size on sensor}}{M} = \frac{0.0052 \text{ mm}}{0.0587}
$$

$$
\text{Object resolution} \approx 0.0886 \text{ mm} = 88.6 \text{ }\mu m
$$

**Marks: 3**

**Engineering Interpretation:** This vision setup can detect features as small as **88.6 $\mu$m**, suitable for inspection of machined parts, electronic components, and precision assemblies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the +1 term** in $WD = f(1 + 1/M)$ — students often write $WD = f + M$ (completely wrong formula). This costs **2 marks** instantly.
> - **Unit mismatch** — focal length and sensor size must both be in **mm**; pixel size often given in $\mu$m → convert first.
> - **In Question A(b),** students often describe vision applications generically. Always specify the **industry, inspection type, technique, and measurable benefit** to score full marks.
> - **In Part A answers,** writing only "list of three" without explanation → loses 1 mark. Always elaborate one application in 2–3 lines.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize before KTU ESE**

- ✅ **Definition:** Machine vision = non-contact, optical, automated inspection.
- ✅ **Six functional categories:** Gauging, Inspection, Recognition, Guidance, Identification, Verification.
- ✅ **Core pipeline:** Acquisition → Enhancement → Feature Extraction → Decision.
- ✅ **Formula — Magnification:** $M = S_w / FOV$.
- ✅ **Formula — Working Distance:** $WD = f \cdot (1 + 1/M)$.
- ✅ **Formula — Centroid:** $x_c = (1/N) \sum x_i$, $y_c = (1/N) \sum y_i$.
- ✅ **Formula — Otsu threshold:** Maximize between-class variance $\sigma_B^2(T)$.
- ✅ **Illumination types:** Backlight, Ring, Bar, Structured Laser, Coaxial, Dome.
- ✅ **Sensor types:** CCD (high quality, expensive), CMOS (fast, cheaper, modern).
- ✅ **Industrial interfaces:** GigE Vision, USB3 Vision, Camera Link, Ethernet/IP, PROFINET.
- ✅ **Automotive applications:** Weld inspection, Bin picking, Paint defect detection, Dimensional gauging.
- ✅ **Pharma applications:** Blister pack verification, Tablet counting, Vial cap inspection.
- ✅ **Robotic guidance types:** Eye-in-hand vs Eye-to-hand configurations.
- ✅ **Typical accuracies:** 0.05 mm (precision gauging) to 0.5 mm (general inspection).
- ✅ **Inspection rate benchmarks:** Up to 10,000 parts/min for high-speed lines.
- ✅ **70% rule:** ~70% of vision project failures are due to **poor illumination design**, not algorithms.
- ✅ **Pattern matching metric:** Normalized cross-correlation $R_{xy} \in [0,1]$; threshold typically 0.7–0.9.
- ✅ **3D vision methods:** Stereo vision, Laser triangulation, Time-of-Flight, Structured light.
- ✅ **Industry standards:** AIA (Automated Imaging Association), EMVA 1288 (sensor characterization).

---

**END OF NOTES — MODULE 4 / MACHINE VISION APPLICATIONS**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech PEMRT633*

topics.title: Flexible Manufacturing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT633 - ADVANCED AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flexible Manufacturing Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** is a highly automated, integrated group technology machine cell, controlled by a distributed computer network, designed to simultaneously produce a variety of parts (in low to medium batch quantities) with minimal manual intervention. It combines **Computer Numerical Control (CNC) machine tools**, automated **Material Handling Systems (MHS)**, **Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (AS/RS)**, and a **Centralized Computer Control System** to achieve high machine utilization, reduced work-in-process (WIP) inventory, and rapid response to product mix changes.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an FMS is a manufacturing system in which a set of machines and tools, linked by an automated material handling system and controlled by a central computer, is capable of producing a family of parts in any order and in any quantity, without significant loss of production time due to changeovers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** An FMS is **NOT** a fully automatic, lights-out factory. It is an integrated system designed for *flexible* mid-volume production — bridging the gap between Dedicated Mass Production and Job-Shop Manufacturing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large modern hospital kitchen** that prepares meals for hundreds of patients with very different dietary requirements. A traditional kitchen would be like a **dedicated assembly line** — efficient only if every patient wants the same meal. A **job shop** would be a single cook making one meal at a time — flexible but slow. A **Flexible Manufacturing System** is like a **centralized kitchen with multiple smart cooking stations, a conveyor belt linking them, and a digital recipe book.** When a new dietary request arrives, the system automatically re-routes ingredients, switches the station's tools, and adjusts cooking parameters — all without shutting down the whole kitchen. That is precisely what an FMS does on a factory floor.

## 1.3 The Six Classic Types of Manufacturing Flexibility

An FMS derives its name from the **flexibility** it offers. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, this flexibility is classified into six distinct types:

| Flexibility Type | Meaning in Plain English | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine Flexibility** | Ability of a single machine to produce a variety of part types. | A CNC lathe switching between shafts and flanges without retooling. |
| **Process Flexibility** | Ability to produce a given part using multiple machines/processes. | Machining a bracket on either VMC-1 or VMC-2 if one is busy. |
| **Product Flexibility** | Ability to introduce new products or modify existing ones quickly. | Adding a new smartphone case model on the same production line. |
| **Routing Flexibility** | Ability of parts to be routed through multiple alternate paths. | A pallet taking Path A or Path B depending on queue length. |
| **Volume Flexibility** | Ability to operate profitably at different production volumes. | Producing 50 units/month or 5000 units/month on the same system. |
| **Expansion Flexibility** | Ability to expand the system capacity by adding modules. | Adding a new machining center to an existing FMS. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A Part Family refers to a group of parts that share similar design attributes (geometry, material, process routing). FMS achieves flexibility primarily because of **Group Technology (GT) classification** of parts into families.

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Scheme Stated Benefits of FMS

The official KTU 2024 syllabus enumerates the following benefits, which must be memorized verbatim for ESE answers:

- **Higher machine utilization** (typically 70–90 % vs. 30–40 % in conventional systems).
- **Reduced direct labor cost** (60–80 % reduction).
- **Reduced work-in-process (WIP) inventory** by 60–80 %.
- **Shorter manufacturing lead time** (often less than 10 % of conventional time).
- **Greater flexibility in product mix** and design changes.
- **Improved product quality** through closed-loop, computer-controlled processing.
- **Reduced floor space requirement** (compact, integrated layout).

The primary engineering **metrics** used by KTU examiners are: **Cpk ≥ 1.33**, **OEE ≥ 85 %**, **changeover time < 10 min**, and **MTTR < 30 min**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of an FMS

A complete FMS has **four mandatory subsystems**, each of which is examined as a 3-mark or 7-mark question in KTU ESE:

### 2.1.1 Workstation Subsystem (Processing Stations)
These are the actual production machines — typically **CNC machining centers, turning centers, robotic welding cells, or assembly stations**. Each workstation has:
- Its own local **Machine Control Unit (MCU)**.
- A **Pallet interface** for receiving incoming parts.
- A **Local tool magazine** (10–40 tools) feeding a central automated tool delivery system.
- Built-in **sensors** (temperature, vibration, tool wear) for closed-loop quality control.

### 2.1.2 Material Handling and Storage Subsystem (MHS)
This is the "circulatory system" of the FMS. Its primary functions are:
- **Inter-workstation transport** of parts and tooling.
- **Buffer storage** of WIP between operations.
- **Loading/Unloading** of raw stock and finished goods.
- **Tool delivery** to and from tool magazines.

KTU 2024 emphasizes three MHS classes: **Conveyor-based, Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV) based, and Industrial Robot based** systems.

### 2.1.3 Computer Control and Software Subsystem (CIM Layer)
This is the "central nervous system" — a **hierarchical, distributed computer network** typically organized into **3–5 functional levels**:
- **Level 0 — Equipment Level:** PLCs, CNC controllers, robot controllers.
- **Level 1 — Cell Control Level:** Cell controllers, local databases, station dispatching.
- **Level 2 — Area/FMS Control Level:** Production scheduling, routing optimization, tool management.
- **Level 3 — Plant/Shop Floor Control:** Order release, capacity planning, dispatching.
- **Level 4 — Enterprise Level:** MRP-II, ERP, customer order management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The communication between levels follows the **ISO/OSI 7-layer model**, with **MAP (Manufacturing Automation Protocol)** and **Ethernet/IP** being the most common KTU-referenced fieldbus standards.

### 2.1.4 Human Resource Subsystem (Operators and Managers)
Despite full automation, humans are required for:
- System **programming and NC code generation**.
- **Tool presetting and maintenance**.
- **Supervisory control** via Human-Machine Interface (HMI) terminals.
- **Quality assurance and exception handling**.

## 2.2 Types of FMS (KTU 2024 Classification)

| Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC)** | Smallest FMS — 1–2 machines + robot + buffer. | Low-variety, medium-volume parts. |
| **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS proper)** | 3–10 machines + automated MHS + central control. | Mid-variety, mid-volume. |
| **Flexible Transfer Line (FTL)** | Sequential dedicated stations with limited flexibility. | High-volume, low-variety families. |

## 2.3 FMS Layout Configurations (KTU High-Yield Diagram)

Four classical layouts are expected in 14-mark ESE questions:

1. **Inline Layout** — machines arranged in a single row, with a linear conveyor. Best for fixed routing and high-volume families.
2. **L-Shaped Layout** — two perpendicular rows, suitable for square floor plans.
3. **Loop / Circular Layout** — most common; enables **routing flexibility** since parts can take multiple paths around the loop.
4. **Open-Field / Rectangular Layout** — multiple rows accessed by a grid of AGVs; enables **maximum routing flexibility** but requires complex scheduling.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is **exam-critical**. Every formula below has appeared (or is statistically likely to appear) in the KTU 2024 ESE papers. The student must commit these to memory.

| Formula / Term | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | A = Availability, P = Performance, Q = Quality | Overall Equipment Effectiveness |
| $Cpk = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | USL/LSL = Spec Limits, $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = std dev | Process capability index |
| $\eta_{machine} = \dfrac{T_{cutting}}{T_{loading} + T_{cutting} + T_{idle} + T_{tool-change}}$ | Ratio of productive cutting time to total time | Machine utilization efficiency |
| $T_{throughput} = T_{longest} + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} T_{buffer,i}$ | $T_{longest}$ = bottleneck operation, $T_{buffer}$ = inter-station buffer | Cycle time of a 1-piece flow line |
| $N_{balance} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{t_{max}}$ | $t_i$ = station times, $t_{max}$ = bottleneck | Line balancing number |
| $\eta_{balance} = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N_{actual} \cdot t_{max}}$ | Measures how well work is distributed | Balance efficiency / line efficiency |
| $WIP_{Little} = TH \cdot CT$ | TH = throughput, CT = cycle time | Little's Law — WIP estimation |
| $P_{down} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - q_i)$ | $q_i$ = failure probability of component *i* | System reliability (series) |
| $T_{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | $\mu$ = repair rate | Mean Time To Repair |
| $T_{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate | Mean Time Between Failures |
| $A_{system} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Combined availability | System availability / uptime |
| $n_{servers} = \rho + z_{\alpha}\sqrt{\rho}$ | M/M/1 queue, $\rho$ = utilization | FMS station server count (M/M/1) |
| $L_q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$ | M/M/1 mean queue length | Queue length before station |
| $W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)}$ | M/M/1 mean waiting time | Part waiting time in FMS queue |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When the question asks "Discuss the FMS planning process," the examiner expects the student to mention: (1) Part family formation, (2) Machine grouping, (3) Layout selection, (4) Tool management, (5) Material handling route planning, and (6) Production scheduling.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

FMS is the operational foundation of **Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing**. In production engineering practice, FMS is the physical layer on which **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, **Digital Twins**, and **AI-driven predictive maintenance** are built. Automotive plants (e.g., engine block machining lines), aerospace component manufacturers, and consumer electronics assembly all rely on FMS architectures to achieve **mass customization**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — FMS Machine Utilization

**Problem Statement:** A 4-machine FMS is observed over a 480-minute shift. Machine 1 was cutting for 360 min, Machine 2 for 300 min, Machine 3 for 240 min, and Machine 4 for 200 min. Idle times were 60, 90, 120, and 180 min respectively, and tool-change times were 60, 90, 120, and 100 min. Compute the system-wide average machine utilization.

### Step 1 — Define the formula
The KTU 2024 formula for utilization per machine is:

$$
\eta_{i} = \frac{T_{cutting,i}}{T_{shift} - T_{down,i}}
$$

In this problem, we assume $T_{down,i} = 0$ (all stations are available during the shift). The "denominator" represents the **scheduled productive time**.

### Step 2 — Compute the scheduled time per machine
Since the shift is **480 min** and there is no downtime, the scheduled time per machine is:

$$
T_{scheduled} = 480 \text{ min}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute each machine's utilization
Substitute the given values into the formula:

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{360}{480} = 0.75 \quad (= 75.00 \%)
$$

$$
\eta_2 = \frac{300}{480} = 0.625 \quad (= 62.50 \%)
$$

$$
\eta_3 = \frac{240}{480} = 0.50 \quad (= 50.00 \%)
$$

$$
\eta_4 = \frac{200}{480} \approx 0.4167 \quad (= 41.67 \%)
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the system-wide average utilization
The average utilization is the arithmetic mean:

$$
\bar{\eta} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\eta_i
$$

$$
\bar{\eta} = \frac{0.75 + 0.625 + 0.50 + 0.4167}{4}
$$

$$
\bar{\eta} = \frac{2.2917}{4} \approx 0.5729
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to percentage
$$
\bar{\eta} \approx 57.29 \%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reason the system-wide utilization is **lower than individual machine utilization** is **load imbalance**. KTU 2024 expects students to identify the **bottleneck machine** (here, Machine 4) and recommend re-allocation of work in their 14-mark answers.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — FMS Throughput Time (Little's Law)

**Problem Statement:** An FMS produces 20 parts/hour on average. The average number of parts in the system (WIP) is observed to be 40. Compute the **average cycle time** of a part.

### Step 1 — State Little's Law
Little's Law (1961) is foundational in FMS analysis:

$$
WIP = TH \cdot CT \quad \Longrightarrow \quad CT = \frac{WIP}{TH}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert throughput to consistent units
TH = 20 parts/hour. Convert to per-minute:

$$
TH = \frac{20}{60} = 0.3333 \text{ parts/min}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute
$$
CT = \frac{40}{0.3333}
$$

$$
CT = 120 \text{ minutes}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
The part spends an average of **120 minutes** in the FMS — from raw material entry to finished part exit. This is the **average lead time**, and is independent of part complexity (it is a system-level property).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — FMS System Availability (Reliability Series Model)

**Problem Statement:** A workstation has $MTBF = 200$ hours and $MTTR = 4$ hours. The FMS has 4 such workstations in series. Compute the **system availability** assuming $\lambda_i = \lambda$ and $\mu_i = \mu$ for all stations.

### Step 1 — Single-station availability
$$
A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{200}{200 + 4} = \frac{200}{204} \approx 0.9804
$$

### Step 2 — Series system availability
For $N$ stations in series, the system availability is the product of individual availabilities:

$$
A_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{N} A_i
$$

$$
A_{sys} = (0.9804)^{4}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate
$$
A_{sys} = 0.9234 \approx 92.34 \%
$$

### Step 4 — Alternative equivalent computation
Equivalently, compute system MTBF and MTTR:

$$
MTBF_{sys} = \frac{MTBF_{single}}{N} = \frac{200}{4} = 50 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
MTTR_{sys} = \frac{MTTR_{single}}{N} = \frac{4}{4} = 1 \text{ hour}
$$

$$
A_{sys} = \frac{50}{50 + 1} = \frac{50}{51} \approx 0.9804
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two methods give slightly different numerical answers because of assumptions about repair parallelism. The product method is the KTU-preferred approach.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation 4 — Line Balancing for FMS Cell Design

**Problem Statement:** A manufacturing cell must perform 6 tasks with the following times (in minutes) and precedence constraints:

| Task | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time | 4 | 6 | 5 | 3 | 7 | 4 |
| Predecessors | — | A | A | B | B,C | D,E |

Design a 2-station cell and compute the **balance efficiency**.

### Step 1 — Total work content
$$
T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} t_i = 4 + 6 + 5 + 3 + 7 + 4 = 29 \text{ min}
$$

### Step 2 — Lower bound on cycle time
The cycle time for a 2-station cell is bounded below by:

$$
CT_{min} = \frac{T_{total}}{N_{stations}} = \frac{29}{2} = 14.5 \text{ min}
$$

### Step 3 — Feasible assignment respecting precedence
A feasible assignment is:

**Station 1:** A (4) + B (6) + C (5) = 15 min
**Station 2:** D (3) + E (7) + F (4) = 14 min

### Step 4 — Determine bottleneck cycle time
$$
CT = \max(15, 14) = 15 \text{ min}
$$

### Step 5 — Balance efficiency
$$
\eta_{balance} = \frac{T_{total}}{N_{stations} \cdot CT} = \frac{29}{2 \cdot 15}
$$

$$
\eta_{balance} = \frac{29}{30} \approx 0.9667 \quad (= 96.67 \%)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Balance efficiency above 95 % is considered **excellent** in FMS design. Anything below 80 % suggests the cell needs restructuring.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — FMS vs. Other Systems

| Parameter | Dedicated Mass Production | Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) | Job Shop |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volume | High | Medium | Low |
| Variety | Very Low | Medium-High | Very High |
| Lead Time | Long setup, short run | Short setup, short run | Long per part |
| WIP Inventory | High | Low (often < 5 days) | High |
| Capital Investment | Medium | Very High | Low |
| Machine Utilization | 30–50 % | 70–90 % | 20–40 % |
| Direct Labor | High | Very Low | Very High |
| Flexibility | None | 6 types | High but inefficient |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical FMS Computer-Control Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **5-level CIM hierarchy** of a typical FMS, which is a high-frequency topic in KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L4[L4 Enterprise Level - ERP - MRP II - Customer Order Management] --> L3
    L3[L3 Plant Shop Floor Control - Order Release - Capacity Planning - Dispatching] --> L2
    L2[L2 FMS Area Control - Production Scheduling - Routing Optimization - Tool Management] --> L1
    L1[L1 Cell Control - Cell Controller - Local Database - Station Dispatching] --> L0
    L0[L0 Equipment Level - PLCs - CNC Controllers - Robot Controllers]
    L4 -.feedback.-> L3
    L3 -.feedback.-> L2
    L2 -.feedback.-> L1
    L1 -.feedback.-> L0
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic FMS

The following diagram maps the **interactions** between the four mandatory FMS subsystems. It is suitable for both 7-mark and 14-mark ESE answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ASRS[ASRS - Raw Material Storage] -- pallet load --> CB[Conveyor Buffer Station]
    CB -- inbound --> WS1[Workstation 1 - CNC Machining Center]
    CB -- inbound --> WS2[Workstation 2 - CNC Turning Center]
    CB -- inbound --> WS3[Workstation 3 - Grinding Station]
    WS1 -- finished part --> CB
    WS2 -- finished part --> CB
    WS3 -- finished part --> CB
    CB -- outgoing --> ASRS_OUT[ASRS - Finished Goods Buffer]
    AGV[AGV Network - Mobile Material Handling] -.transports.-> CB
    ROB[Industrial Robot Loader] -.pallet exchange.-> WS1
    ROB -.pallet exchange.-> WS2
    ROB -.pallet exchange.-> WS3
    CCS[Central Computer Control System] -- NC programs --> WS1
    CCS -- NC programs --> WS2
    CCS -- NC programs --> WS3
    CCS -- dispatch commands --> AGV
    CCS -- tool requests --> TMS[Tool Management System]
    TMS -- tool delivery --> WS1
    TMS -- tool delivery --> WS2
    TMS -- tool delivery --> WS3
    HMI[Human Machine Interface Terminals] -- operator commands --> CCS
    HMI -- status display --> CCS
```

## 4.3 Routing-Flexibility Comparison Across FMS Layouts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Part Entering FMS] --> B{Choose Layout Based on Routing Flexibility}
    B -- high volume fixed routing --> C[Inline Layout]
    B -- medium variety --> D[Loop Layout]
    B -- square floor plan --> E[L-Shaped Layout]
    B -- maximum routing --> F[Open Field AGV Grid]
    C --> G[Single Path Only]
    D --> H[Multiple Paths Around Loop]
    E --> I[Two Perpendicular Paths]
    F --> J[Any-to-Any Routing via AGV]
```

## 4.4 FMS Planning Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Part Family Formation via GT] --> S2
    S2[Step 2 Machine Grouping and Selection] --> S3
    S3[Step 3 FMS Layout Selection] --> S4
    S4[Step 4 Material Handling System Design] --> S5
    S5[Step 5 Tool Management System Design] --> S6
    S6[Step 6 Production Scheduling and Dispatch] --> S7
    S7[Step 7 Real Time Shop Floor Control]
    S7 -.feedback.-> S6
    S6 -.feedback.-> S5
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS). List any four benefits of FMS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** is an integrated, computer-controlled manufacturing system comprising a group of CNC machine tools, an automated material handling system, and a centralized computer control system, capable of producing a variety of parts in low to medium batch sizes with minimal human intervention.

**Four benefits of FMS:**
1. **Higher machine utilization** (70–90 % vs. 30–40 % in conventional systems).
2. **Reduced work-in-process inventory** by 60–80 %.
3. **Shorter manufacturing lead time** — often less than 10 % of conventional time.
4. **Greater flexibility in product mix** and rapid response to design changes.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between "Routing Flexibility" and "Process Flexibility" in the context of FMS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Routing Flexibility:** The ability of an FMS to route a part through **multiple alternate sequences of workstations** depending on the system's real-time state (e.g., machine availability, queue length). The system dynamically selects the path.
- **Process Flexibility:** The ability to produce a **given part using more than one type of machine or process**, when its primary assigned machine is unavailable. It is essentially redundancy in processing capability.

The key distinction: **routing flexibility** concerns the *order of operations*, while **process flexibility** concerns the *substitutability of machines for a single operation*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the major **components/subsystems of a Flexible Manufacturing System** with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **planning and implementation steps** for setting up an FMS in a manufacturing industry. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The four mandatory subsystems of an FMS are:

**1. Workstation Subsystem (2 marks):**
This consists of the actual production machines — typically CNC machining centers, CNC turning centers, grinding machines, robotic welding cells, or assembly stations. Each workstation has its own Machine Control Unit (MCU), a pallet interface, a local tool magazine (10–40 tools), and sensors for closed-loop process monitoring. Workstations perform the actual value-added operations on the parts.

**2. Material Handling and Storage Subsystem (2 marks):**
This subsystem acts as the circulatory system of the FMS. It includes:
- **Conveyors, Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs), and Industrial Robots** for inter-station transport.
- **Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (AS/RS)** for buffer and finished-goods storage.
- **Pallet changers and load/unload stations** for the physical interface.
Its primary functions are inter-workstation transport, buffer storage of WIP, raw material loading, and tool delivery.

**3. Computer Control and Software Subsystem (2 marks):**
This is the central nervous system — a hierarchical, distributed computer network. It is organized into 5 levels: (i) Equipment level (PLCs, CNC controllers), (ii) Cell control level (cell controllers, local databases), (iii) FMS area control level (production scheduling, tool management, routing), (iv) Plant shop floor control (order release, capacity planning), and (v) Enterprise level (MRP-II, ERP). Communication is achieved through fieldbus standards such as MAP and Ethernet/IP.

**4. Human Resource Subsystem (1 mark):**
Despite automation, humans are required for NC programming, tool presetting, system maintenance, and supervisory control via HMI terminals. Operators also handle exceptions and quality assurance.

*(A neat block diagram showing the four subsystems and their interconnections earns the remaining marks per KTU valuation norms.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

The FMS planning and implementation process is structured into **seven sequential steps**:

**Step 1 — Part Family Formation (1 mark):**
Using Group Technology (GT) classification — typically via the **Opitz coding system** or **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)** — the entire part population is grouped into families that share similar design and process attributes. FMS cannot be justified unless part families are large enough.

**Step 2 — Machine Grouping and Selection (1 mark):**
For each part family, the required machining operations are identified, and a logical group of machines is selected (e.g., 3 CNC VMCs + 1 turning center for a family of brackets and housings). The selection considers part-mix volume, tooling, and accuracy requirements.

**Step 3 — FMS Layout Selection (1 mark):**
The layout is chosen based on volume, variety, and floor space. Common options: **Inline (fixed routing), L-shaped, Loop (routing flexibility), or Open-Field AGV Grid (maximum flexibility).** Loop is the most common.

**Step 4 — Material Handling System Design (1 mark):**
The MHS — conveyors, AGVs, or robots — is selected and routed. AGVs are preferred for high-routing-flexibility systems. The buffer sizes between stations are designed using queuing theory (typically M/M/1 model).

**Step 5 — Tool Management System Design (1 mark):**
A central tool room with a delivery system, a tool data management database, and presetters is designed. The number of tool magazines, the tool delivery time, and the tool-life management policy are all finalized here.

**Step 6 — Production Scheduling and Dispatch Logic (1 mark):**
The scheduling algorithm is selected — typically **Johnson's Rule** for 2-machine problems, **CDS (Campbell, Dudek, Smith) heuristic** for *n*-machine problems, or **NEH (Nawaz-Enscore-Ham) algorithm** for flow shops. Dispatching is based on shortest processing time (SPT) or earliest due date (EDD).

**Step 7 — Implementation, Testing, and Ramp-up (1 mark):**
The system is installed, dry-run with dummy parts, validated using simulation (e.g., Arena, FlexSim), and progressively ramped up to full production with parallel manual lines for risk mitigation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in 14-mark FMS Answers (KTU Examiner Insight):**
> 1. Confusing **FMS with Cellular Manufacturing** — FMS is computer-integrated, Cellular may be manual.
> 2. Forgetting the **human subsystem** in component listings.
> 3. Failing to mention **Group Technology (GT)** in planning steps — it is the foundation of FMS.
> 4. Drawing only one layout type when asked to "compare" layouts.
> 5. Not specifying any **scheduling algorithm** by name in implementation steps.

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What are the different **types of flexibility** provided by an FMS? Explain each in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Group Technology (GT)** and its role in the design of an FMS. Describe the **Opitz classification and coding system** briefly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

An FMS provides **six types of flexibility**, each contributing uniquely to manufacturing agility:

**1. Machine Flexibility (1.5 marks):**
The ability of a single machine to produce a variety of part types without significant setup time. Enabled by CNC, ATC (Automatic Tool Changer), and APC (Automatic Pallet Changer). Example: A VMC producing 50 different bracket types in a single shift.

**2. Process Flexibility (1 mark):**
The ability to produce a given part using multiple machine types or processes. Example: A bracket can be machined on VMC-1 or VMC-2 if one is busy. Process flexibility requires redundant capability and is essential for **system robustness** against machine failure.

**3. Product Flexibility (1 mark):**
The ability to introduce new products or modify existing ones quickly into the existing FMS. Requires only **NC code regeneration** and minor tool additions — no physical reconfiguration. Product flexibility is the most economically valuable type for high-mix industries.

**4. Routing Flexibility (1.5 marks):**
The ability of parts to be routed through multiple alternate workstation sequences based on real-time system state (machine availability, queue length). Requires an automated MHS (AGV or robot) and a central scheduling computer.

**5. Volume Flexibility (1 mark):**
The ability to operate profitably at different production volumes. FMS is unique in that it can scale output from 50 units/month to 5000 units/month on the same equipment without retooling. This is enabled by NC programmability and modular machine cells.

**6. Expansion Flexibility (1 mark):**
The ability to expand the system capacity by adding new machines, workstations, or material handling modules. The system is designed with standardized interfaces so new components can be plugged in with minimal disruption. This is the foundation of **scalable Industry 4.0 factories**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Group Technology (GT) — Concept (3 marks):**
Group Technology is a manufacturing philosophy in which **similar parts are identified and grouped together to take advantage of their similarities in design and manufacturing**. The grouping reduces the variety of parts handled, enabling efficient planning, scheduling, and production.

In FMS design, GT is **foundational** because:
- It identifies **part families** that can be produced on the same FMS cell.
- It reduces the number of tooling changeovers.
- It enables **standardization** of fixtures, tooling, and process plans.
- It allows **cellular layout** design based on flow-line principles.

GT implementation uses two principal methods:
1. **Production Flow Analysis (PFA)** — analyzes process routings and groups parts with similar routes.
2. **Classification and Coding Systems** — assigns alphanumeric codes to parts based on design and manufacturing attributes.

**Opitz Classification and Coding System (4 marks):**
Developed by **H. Opitz** in Germany (1970s), this is the most widely used GT coding system globally and is the KTU-recommended system.

The Opitz code is a **9-digit alphanumeric string** of the form:

$$
\text{Opitz Code} = \underbrace{D_1 D_2 D_3 D_4}_{Form} \; \underbrace{D_5}_{Material} \; \underbrace{D_6 D_7 D_8 D_9}_{Process}
$$

**Digits 1–4 (Form Code):** Describe the **geometric form** of the part.
- D1: External shape (rotational, prismatic, flat, etc.) — **5 categories**
- D2: External features (threads, gear teeth, etc.) — **6 categories**
- D3: Internal shape (bore, blind hole, etc.) — **5 categories**
- D4: Internal features (threads, grooves, etc.) — **6 categories**

**Digit 5 (Material Code):** Describes the raw material — ferrous, non-ferrous, plastic, etc. — **5 categories**.

**Digits 6–9 (Process Code):** Describe the **manufacturing processes** required.
- D6: Primary shaping (casting, forging, etc.) — **5 categories**
- D7: Secondary shaping (turning, milling, etc.) — **5 categories**
- D8: Tertiary processes (grinding, etc.) — **5 categories**
- D9: Joining/finishing (welding, painting, etc.) — **4 categories**

Each digit is assigned a value from 0 to 9 (sometimes 0–7). Parts with **identical first 5 digits and similar last 4 digits** belong to the same GT family.

**Example:** Opitz code 0110 2 3210 denotes:
- Rotational part (D1=0), with external thread (D2=1), with through bore (D3=1), no internal features (D4=0),
- Made of steel (D5=2),
- No primary shaping (D6=3), turning + milling (D7=2), grinding (D8=1), no joining (D9=0).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** KTU examiners specifically **deduct marks** when students:
> 1. Confuse the Opitz code with the **DCLASS system** (which has a different structure).
> 2. Forget the **9-digit length** of the Opitz code.
> 3. State that the Opitz code classifies **processes only** (it classifies both form and process).
> 4. Fail to mention the role of GT in **part family formation** as a prerequisite for FMS design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Flexible Manufacturing Systems**

- [ ] **Definition:** FMS = CNC machines + automated MHS + central computer + humans, producing part families.
- [ ] **Six Flexibilities:** Machine, Process, Product, Routing, Volume, Expansion.
- [ ] **Four Subsystems:** Workstation, MHS, Computer Control, Human.
- [ ] **5-Level CIM Hierarchy:** L0 (Equipment) → L1 (Cell) → L2 (FMS) → L3 (Plant) → L4 (Enterprise).
- [ ] **Three FMS Types:** FMC (smallest), FMS (medium), FTL (largest).
- [ ] **Four Layouts:** Inline, L-shaped, Loop, Open-Field AGV Grid.
- [ ] **GT Foundation:** Part family formation via **Opitz code** (9 digits: 4 Form + 1 Material + 4 Process).
- [ ] **OEE formula:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ — target ≥ 85 %.
- [ ] **Cpk formula:** $Cpk = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ — target ≥ 1.33.
- [ ] **Little's Law:** $WIP = TH \times CT$ — applies to FMS steady state.
- [ ] **Availability:** $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — series system: $A_{sys} = \prod A_i$.
- [ ] **Line Balance Efficiency:** $\eta_{balance} = \frac{\sum t_i}{N \cdot CT}$ — target ≥ 95 %.
- [ ] **M/M/1 Queue:** $L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$ and $W_q = \frac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)}$ for FMS station analysis.
- [ ] **Benefits to memorize (verbatim):** Higher utilization, lower WIP, shorter lead time, lower labor, higher quality, less floor space.
- [ ] **Industry 4.0 link:** FMS is the **physical layer** of smart manufacturing — Digital Twins, CPS, and AI predictive maintenance are built on top of FMS.
- [ ] **Common exam traps:** Confusing FMS with FMC or FTL; forgetting human subsystem; missing GT in planning; using wrong scheduling algorithm.
- [ ] **Top KTU-referenced scheduling heuristics:** SPT, EDD, Johnson's Rule (2-machine), NEH (*n*-machine), CDS heuristic.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Flexible Manufacturing Systems**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — PEMRT633 Advanced Automation*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT633 - ADVANCED AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of FMS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an FMS? (Foundational Definition)

A **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** is a highly automated, computer-controlled configuration of machine tools, material handling devices, and inspection stations linked by an integrated material and information flow system. It is designed to produce a **family of parts** (or parts within a part family) in any quantity, in any order, with minimal manual intervention.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Reference):**
> An FMS is an integrated, computer-controlled complex of numerically controlled (NC) machine tools, automated material handling equipment, and a central supervisory computer system, capable of simultaneous production of multiple part types at varying volumes.

The flexibility of such a system is broadly classified along **three dimensions**:

| Flexibility Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine Flexibility** | Ability to produce new part types | Changing part program |
| **Routing Flexibility** | Ability to handle breakdowns / alternate path | Re-routing jobs around a failed machine |
| **Production Flexibility** | Ability to change production volume | Increasing/decreasing batch sizes |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Modular Smart Factory"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine two kitchens preparing 10 different dishes.
> - **Traditional Kitchen (Dedicated System):** Each stove is permanently fixed to one recipe. To cook Biryani, you must use Stove #3, which only cooks Biryani. Changeover takes hours.
> - **Modular Smart Kitchen (FMS):** Every stove is identical (CNC machine), all ingredients are auto-fetched from a smart pantry (AS/RS), and a central AI (Supervisory Computer) decides which stove cooks what, in what order, automatically. Robots (AGVs) move pans (pallets) between stoves. You can run 10 dishes simultaneously, change recipes hourly, and if Stove #5 breaks, the AI redistributes work to Stoves 1–4 — no downtime.

This analogy perfectly explains why FMS exists: **variety + volume + agility** — without the rigidity of a transfer line.

### 1.3 Why Do We Need "Types" of FMS?

Not every factory needs a 50-machine mega-system. The **scale, part variety, and budget** dictate which type of FMS is best. KTU Module 4 categorizes FMS into **five canonical types**, each suiting a specific production profile.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMRT633 Module 4):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly lists the following as examinable types: **Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC)**, **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)**, **Flexible Transfer Line (FTL)**, **Distributed FMS**, and **Flexible Manufacturing Module (FMM)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Canonical Types of FMS (KTU Mandated Taxonomy)

#### Type 1 — Flexible Manufacturing Module (FMM)

The **FMM** is the smallest, most elementary unit of flexible automation. It is essentially **one CNC machine tool + a local parts buffer + a robot loader**, functioning as a self-contained, standalone work cell.

> **Why it exists:** A factory needing just *one* automated workhorse (e.g., a single machining center loading its own parts) does not need the overhead of a full FMS. The FMM gives entry-level flexibility.

- **Configuration:** 1 CNC + 1 Robot + 1–2 Pallets + Local Buffer
- **Worker Level:** 1 operator (or unattended for hours)
- **Typical Volume:** Low-to-medium batches
- **Flexibility:** Only machine flexibility (no routing flexibility)

#### Type 2 — Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC)

The **FMC** is a small cluster of **2–4 CNC machines** served by a shared material handler (robot or AGV) and a **local cell controller**. It produces a **part family** — parts with similar geometry requiring similar processes.

> **Why it exists:** When you need part-family flexibility but cannot afford a full factory-wide FMS, the FMC is the sweet spot. It is the **most popular FMS type** in industry worldwide.

- **Configuration:** 2–4 CNCs + 1–2 Robots/AGVs + Central Pallet Pool + Cell Computer
- **Worker Level:** 1 operator supervises multiple machines
- **Typical Volume:** Medium batches, 50–2000 parts
- **Flexibility:** Machine + Routing flexibility

#### Type 3 — Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) — The Full System

This is the **canonical, "textbook" FMS**. It integrates **4 or more CNC workstations**, an automated material handling system (AGVs + conveyors), an **automated storage and retrieval system (AS/RS)**, a central tool management system, and a **supervisory computer** managing the entire production.

> **Why it exists:** Large factories with high part variety and need for unmanned "lights-out" production adopt the FMS.

- **Configuration:** 4–20+ CNCs + AGV Network + AS/RS + Tool Magazines + DNC + Supervisory Computer
- **Worker Level:** Operators only monitor (no direct machining)
- **Typical Volume:** High mix, medium-to-high volume
- **Flexibility:** Machine + Routing + Production flexibility (complete)

#### Type 4 — Flexible Transfer Line (FTL)

The **FTL** (sometimes called a **Dedicated FMS**) is a **hybrid** — it combines the rigidity and high speed of a classical transfer line with the programmability of CNC machines. It is designed for a **specific part family** that must be produced at very high volumes with periodic model changes.

> **Why it exists:** Automotive engine plants (e.g., a plant making 4 variants of an engine block) use FTLs. The line is dedicated to a part family but each station is CNC, so minor design changes can be absorbed.

- **Configuration:** Series of dedicated CNC stations linked by a fixed transfer mechanism (pallet conveyor or walking beam)
- **Worker Level:** 1–2 supervisors for the entire line
- **Typical Volume:** Very high (10,000+ parts/month per family)
- **Flexibility:** Production flexibility only (model change every few months)

#### Type 5 — Distributed / Hierarchical FMS

A **Distributed FMS** is a network of geographically separated cells or systems, linked by a **central coordinating computer** via LAN/WAN. Each subsystem may be a FMC or FMM; the central computer handles order dispatch, load balancing, and inter-cell transfers (usually via truck or inter-site AGV).

> **Why it exists:** Multi-plant companies (e.g., a company with 3 factories across Kerala/Tamil Nadu) need enterprise-wide coordination.

- **Configuration:** Multiple FMCs/FMMs + Inter-site Communication Network + Enterprise Scheduler
- **Worker Level:** Distributed — each site has its own crew + a central planner
- **Typical Volume:** Enterprise-wide
- **Flexibility:** All three flexibilities + geographic flexibility

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table

| Attribute | FMM | FMC | FMS | FTL | Distributed FMS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of Machines** | 1 | 2–4 | 4–20+ | 6–30+ (dedicated) | Many (across sites) |
| **Material Handling** | Robot / fixed loader | Robot / shared AGV | AGV network + conveyor | Fixed transfer mechanism | AGVs + trucks + network |
| **Controller Scope** | Local | Cell-level | Shop-floor level | Line-level | Enterprise-level |
| **Investment (Indicative)** | Lowest | Moderate | High | High | Highest |
| **Flexibility Level** | Low | Medium | Highest (in unit) | Medium-High | Highest (enterprise) |
| **Typical Industry** | Job shops | SME machining | Aerospace, Defence | Automotive | Conglomerates |
| **KTU Marks Weightage** | Low | **Very High** | **Very High** | Medium | Low |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quantitative Sizing of an FMS

These formulas are essential for KTU 14-mark numerical/analytical questions.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Variables | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{p} = T_{p} \times R_{h} \times E$ | $N_{p}$ = number of pallet fixtures; $T_{p}$ = process time (h); $R_{h}$ = hourly production rate (parts/h); $E$ = efficiency factor | Sizing the pallet pool of an FMC |
| 2 | $N_{m} = \dfrac{R_{h} \times T_{p}}{60 \times \eta \times U}$ | $N_{m}$ = required machines; $\eta$ = machine efficiency; $U$ = utilization | Sizing the number of CNC machines |
| 3 | $N_{w} = \dfrac{T_{total}}{t_{load} + t_{unload}}$ | $N_{w}$ = number of workers; $T_{total}$ = total work content; $t_{load}$ = loading time | Workforce planning |
| 4 | $TH = \dfrac{1}{T_{bot}}$ | $TH$ = throughput (parts/h); $T_{bot}$ = bottleneck station time | Line balancing in FTL |
| 5 | $T_{cyc} = t_{mach} + t_{handle} + t_{wait}$ | $T_{cyc}$ = cycle time of one part through the FMS | FMS performance benchmarking |
| 6 | $C_{payback} = \dfrac{C_{capex}}{S_{annual} - C_{opex}}$ | $C_{capex}$ = capital expenditure; $S_{annual}$ = annual savings; $C_{opex}$ = annual operating cost | Justifying FMS investment |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule for Tables:** Notice that all absolute-value notations use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown table grammar. Do NOT use `|x|` directly inside any table row.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Number of Machines Required for an FMC

This is the most-asked KTU numerical. Given a target production rate, determine how many CNC machines are needed.

**Problem Statement:**
An FMC must produce **120 parts per day** in an **8-hour shift**. Each part requires **4 minutes** of machining. Machine efficiency $\eta = 0.85$ and utilization $U = 0.90$. Calculate the number of machines required.

**Step 1 — Identify the target hourly production rate.**

$$
R_{h} = \dfrac{120 \text{ parts}}{8 \text{ h}} = 15 \text{ parts/h}
$$

*Logic:* Total parts divided by total available hours gives the per-hour demand that the FMC must sustain.

**Step 2 — Convert the process time to hours for unit consistency.**

$$
T_{p} = \dfrac{4 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ min/h}} = \dfrac{1}{15} \text{ h/part} \approx 0.0667 \text{ h/part}
$$

*Logic:* Since $R_{h}$ is in parts/hour, the per-part time must also be in hours to keep units balanced.

**Step 3 — Apply the standard machine-sizing formula.**

$$
N_{m} = \dfrac{R_{h} \times T_{p}}{\eta \times U}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
N_{m} = \dfrac{15 \times 0.0667}{0.85 \times 0.90}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
N_{m} = \dfrac{1.0005}{0.765}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
0.85 \times 0.90 = 0.765
$$

**Step 7 — Final division.**

$$
N_{m} = \dfrac{1.0005}{0.765} \approx 1.308
$$

**Step 8 — Round up to the next whole integer (because you cannot buy 0.3 of a machine).**

$$
N_{m} = 2 \text{ machines}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Although the math says 1.3 machines, we round **up** to 2 to handle breakdowns, tool changes, and shift variability. This is a standard KTU examiner expectation.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right FMS Type for a Scenario

| Production Scenario | Recommended FMS Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| A small workshop producing 5 part types, low volume | **FMM** | Single-machine automation is sufficient and cost-effective |
| A medium factory making 30 part types, medium volume | **FMC** | Best balance of flexibility and cost; 2–4 machines |
| An aerospace plant with 100+ part types, high mix | **Full FMS** | Routing flexibility is critical; AS/RS needed |
| An automotive engine plant, 4 engine variants, high volume | **FTL** | Dedicated line with CNC stations for high throughput |
| A multi-plant company across Kerala/Tamil Nadu/Karnataka | **Distributed FMS** | Enterprise-wide coordination via central scheduler |

### 3.3 Decision Tree for FMS Type Selection

The following matrix maps a factory's needs to the correct FMS type:

$$
\text{Decision Flow} = \begin{cases}
\text{FMM} & \text{if part\_count} \leq 10 \text{ AND volume is low} \\
\text{FMC} & \text{if } 10 < \text{part\_count} \leq 50 \text{ AND volume is medium} \\
\text{FMS} & \text{if part\_count} > 50 \text{ AND variety is high} \\
\text{FTL} & \text{if part\_count} \leq 5 \text{ AND volume is very high (same family)} \\
\text{Distributed FMS} & \text{if multiple sites AND enterprise integration needed}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 14-mark descriptive answers, students are often asked: *"A factory wants to produce 50 part types in batches of 200. Recommend an FMS type and justify."* The decision tree above is the model answer's backbone.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchical Architecture of FMS Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Automation Need Identified] --> nodeB{Number of Part Types?}
    nodeB --> nodeC[1 to 10 Types] --> nodeD[Low Volume?] --> nodeE[FMM - Flexible Manufacturing Module]
    nodeB --> nodeF[10 to 50 Types] --> nodeG[Medium Volume?] --> nodeH[FMC - Flexible Manufacturing Cell]
    nodeB --> nodeI[More Than 50 Types] --> nodeJ[High Variety?] --> nodeK[FMS - Full Flexible Manufacturing System]
    nodeB --> nodeL[Less Than 5 Types in Same Family] --> nodeM[Very High Volume?] --> nodeN[FTL - Flexible Transfer Line]
    nodeB --> nodeO[Multiple Geographic Sites] --> nodeP[Distributed FMS - Enterprise Network]

    nodeE -.-> nodeQ[1 CNC + 1 Robot + Local Buffer]
```
